https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-50564-x
Nature Communications|   (2024) 15:6321 
6

and pericytes/vSMCs at 5 dpi and 14 dpi, but the Pdgfb-Pdgfrb inter-
action between pericytes/vSMCs and microglia/macrophages was
stronger at 14 dpi (Fig. 5E, F). This suggested that there were different
interactions between microglia/macrophages and ﬁbroblasts and
pericytes/vSMCs at the early and late stages after SCI. We also detected
interactions between Schwann cells (SCs) and pericytes/vSMCs and
between Schwann cells and ﬁbroblasts through Pdgfb-Pdgfrb at 14 dpi,
and the ISLRP of Pdgfb-Pdgfrb between pericytes/vSMCs and SC was
higher than that between ﬁbroblasts and SCs (Fig. 5E, F). Collectively,
our data suggested that the TGF-β and Wnt signaling pathways are
primarily activated in ﬁbroblasts and participate in ﬁbrosis, while the
PDGFB/PDGFD signaling pathway is more highly activated in peri-
cytes/vSMCs.
We also examined the activation of TGF-β, Wnt and PDGF signal-
ing pathways in other cell types (Fig. S10). Overall, the expression level
of ligand and receptor genes were higher in ﬁbroblasts compared with
other cell types. In addition, TGF-β signaling and Wnt signaling were
activated in Schwann cells and endothelial cells, and Wnt signaling was
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-50564-x
Nature Communications|   (2024) 15:6321 
7

also activated in astrocytes. It suggests the possible contribution of
Schwann cells, endothelial cells and astrocytes for ﬁbrotic scar.
Furthermore, we analyzed expression patterns of ligand and
receptor genes in CE-F and LA-F (Fig. 5G, H). Tgfb1, Tgfb2 and Tgfb3
were higher expressed in CE-F than in LA-F, while corresponding
receptor genes were higher expressed in LA-F. The expression levels of
receptor genes of PDGF signaling pathway in LA-F were higher than
that in CE-F. LA-F expressed higher levels of Wnt pathway ligand genes
Fzd2 than CE-F, while CE-F expressed higher level of Fzd1. It suggests
TGF-β, PDGF and Wnt pathway are differently activated in LA-F and
CE-F.
The heterogeneity of ﬁbroblasts in ﬁbrotic scars after SCI is
conserved between mice and monkeys
To further verify the results in mice, we established a transection SCI
model of Macaca mulatta, and dissected the lesion edge and lesion
core tissues at different time points after SCI for 10× Genomics
sequencing and analysis (Fig. 6A). Canonical marker genes for endo-
thelial cells (PECAM1 and CLDN5), pericytes/vSMCs (RGS5 and KCNJ8),
ﬁbroblasts (DCN and COL1A1), and dividing ﬁbroblasts (MKI67 and
TOP2A) were identiﬁed according to expression pattern and used to
perform cluster analysis (Fig. 6B, C). Dividing ﬁbroblasts were
observed at 7 dpi and subsequently decreased in abundance (Fig. 6D).
The expression levels of ECM-related genes were altered post injury
(Fig. 6E). TNC and CTHRC1A, which promote ECM deposition, initially
increased and then decreased post injury (Fig. 6E). FN1 expression was
higher post injury, while the expression levels of LAMB2 and other
laminin subunit genes were lower after SCI (Fig. 6E). These changes in
these genes promoting ECM deposition were similar to those observed
in mice. Notably, COL12A1 expression persistently decreased after SCI
in monkeys (Fig. 6E). The expression of genes inhibiting ECM deposi-
tion POSTN and MFAP2, remained at a high level until 30 dpi, then
dropped to pre-injury levels (Fig. 6E). Collectively, these observations
indicate that ﬁbroblasts express both genes promoting and inhibiting
ECM deposition after monkey SCI.
It should be noted that, to distinguish between MFs and PFs in
monkey spinal cord, we removed the meninges of normal spinal cord
prior to scRNA-seq. Only one small cluster of ﬁbroblasts, cluster 4, was
observed at 0 dpi, suggesting that cluster 4 contains PFs but not MFs.
Cluster 4 cells in the lesion core after SCI were also identiﬁed as PFs,
while cluster 0 in lesion edge were MFs (Fig. 6D). At 14 dpi and 30 dpi,
most of the ﬁbroblasts at the lesion edge were PFs (Fig. 6D), which
indicating that PFs at the edge and MFs in the center of the ﬁbrotic scar
(Fig. 6D). This conclusion was also conﬁrmed by the expression pat-
terns of the DEGs obtained from mouse ﬁbroblasts in macaque ﬁbro-
blasts (Fig. 6F). Consistent with observations in mice, monkey MFs
expressed higher levels of CRABP2, EMB, COL1A1, COL1A2, and FN1,
while PFs expressed collagen IV and laminin subunit-related gene
LAMA2 (Fig. 6F and Fig. S11), which can be also denoted as CE-F and LA-
F, respectively. We also found more CE-F were recruited to the lesion
core in monkeys than in mice, suggesting larger injury area leading to
more MFs inﬁltrated (Fig. 6G). Immunostaining indicated that
CRABP2+ signals was located in the center and LAMA1+ signals at the
edge of the ﬁbrotic scar, in line with the phenomenon observed in mice
(Fig. 6H). In sum, heterogeneity of ﬁbroblasts in ﬁbrotic scar tissue
after SCI is observed in both mice and monkeys.
LA-F participate in lipid transport function after spinal
cord injury
SCI often leads to a notable accumulation of lipids derived from
myelin debris that can be observed in the lesion, extending well
beyond the epicenter49,50. We analyzed the function of ﬁbroblasts on
lipid metabolism. CE-F highly expressed genes related to cholesterol
synthesis (Hmgcr, Pmvk, Hmgcs1), while LA-F highly expressed genes
related to cholesterol transport (Apod, Apoe, Abca1 and Lrp1)
(Fig. 7A). After SCI, lipid droplets (LDs) were found. In PDGFRβ-
CreER::R26-TdT mice, the proportion of LA-F containing Bodipy+ LDs
was signiﬁcantly higher than that of CE-F (Fig. 7B, C). We also isolated
and cultured rodent CE-F from dura/arachnoid and LA-F from pia in
vitro. Consistent with the results described above, CE-F highly
expressed Emb, Crabp2, Fn1 and Col1a1, and LA-F highly expressed
Lama1, Lama2 and Apod (Fig. 7D). Additionally, in vitro experiments
conﬁrmed that CE-F highly expressed genes related to cholesterol
synthesis (Hmgcr, Pmvk, Hmgcs1), while LA-F highly expressed genes
related to cholesterol transport (Apoe and Abca1), esteriﬁcation
(Soat1) and hydrolysis (Lipa) (Fig. 7E). When incubating ﬁbroblasts
with myelin sheaths, the proportion of LA-F that phagocytosed
myelin and then formed LDs was also higher than CE-F, corre-
sponding to in vivo results (Fig. 7F, G). Together, it suggests LA-F has
lipid transport function both in vitro and in vivo.
LA-F play a signiﬁcant role in promoting vascular formation
compared to CE-F
We performed GO enrichment analysis of the DEGs between mouse
CE-F and LA-F. Both CE-F and LA-F shared enriched biological process
terms related to ECM organization, whereas the top enriched biolo-
gical processes for LA-F pertained to vascular development and
angiogenesis (Fig. 8A). Immunostaining for Lama1 and Pdx in the
transection and crush SCI models showed that Pdx+ signals were
surrounded by Lama1+ signals, suggesting that LA-F supported the
functions of vessels in the lesion core (Fig. 8B). To further investigate
the role of CE-F and LA-F on angiogenesis, rodent ﬁbroblasts and
endothelial cells were co-cultured using transwell. Compared to CE-F,
LA-F promoted the formation of blood vessels in vitro (Fig. 8C). In
monkeys, we also performed GO enrichment analysis of the DEGs
between LA-F and CE-F in the lesion core and found that LA-F were
associated with blood vessel and vasculature development (Fig. 8D).
The Pdx+ endothelial cells were surrounded by Lama1+ LA-F, which
was consistent with the observations in mice (Fig. 8E). The interaction
between LA-F and endothelial cells was stronger than that between CE-
F and endothelial cells (Fig. 8F). Overall, these results suggest that
perivascular/pial LA-F contributes to vessel formation after SCI.
Fig. 3 | Scar-forming ﬁbroblasts were derived from MFs and PFs with different
spatial source. A UMAP plots showing the expression patterns of speciﬁc markers
for perivascular (PF) and meningeal (MF) ﬁbroblasts. B UMAP of cluster analysis
revealed 10 clusters of ﬁbroblasts (cluster 1, 2, 6, 7), dividing ﬁbroblasts (cluster 9),
and pericytes/vSMCs (cluster 0, 3, 4, 5, 8). C The proportion of each cluster at 0 dpi,
5 dpi, and 14 dpi. Cluster 9 (dividing ﬁbroblasts) and cluster 2 emerged after injury.
D Expression patterns of the top 20 DEGs between clusters 1 and 6 in ﬁbroblasts
showing the similarity of cluster 2 and 6. E Heatmap indicating stronger relation-
ship between cluster 2 and cluster 6. Five-hundred genes with high standard
deviation in average expression analysis are selected to do Spearman’s correlation.
F Integrated UMAP plot of ﬁbroblasts from our transection SCI data and published
crush SCI data31,46. G–I UMAP plot showing the expression patterns of PF markers
Lama1 (G) and Slc1a3 (H) and MF marker Emb (I) in crush SCI and transection SCI.
J The expression patterns of Emb and Lama1 in PDGFRβ-CreER::R26-TdT mice. The
immunoﬂuorescence signal of Lama1 is located in the pia that inside of meninges
and in the parenchyma, while Emb is mainly expressed in the meninges outside of
Lama1. Three repeats are performed independently. Dashed lines distinguish dura/
arachnoid and pia mater. Scale bar: 50 μm. K The expression patterns of Crabp2
and Lama2 in Col1a2-CreER::R26-TdT mice. The immunoﬂuorescence signal of
Lama2 is located in the pia that inside of meninges and in the parenchyma, while
Crabp2 is exclusively expressed in the outer dura matter or arachnoid. Three
repeats are performed independently. Dashed lines distinguish dura/arachnoid and
pia mater. Scale bar: 50 μm. Source data are provided as a Source Data ﬁle.
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-50564-x
Nature Communications|   (2024) 15:6321 
8

Discussion
It has been reported that ﬁbrotic scars originate from both MFs and
PFs in a dorsal hemisection model where the dura is torn15. In contrast,
after contusive SCI, where the dura remains intact, the ﬁbrotic scar
originates from PFs instead of MFs15. Our results demonstrate a dual
source of PDGFRβ+ cells in the lesion core after either penetrating or
non-penetrating SCI, regardless of whether the dura is intact. Crush
(penetrating) and transection (non-penetrating) SCI give rise to similar
patterns of PDGFRβ-derived cells, despite the relatively lower density
of PDGFRβ+ cells in crush SCI compared with transection SCI. We
found the minimal contribution of pericytes/vSMCs to ﬁbrotic scar
formation. There were differences in the distribution, cellular char-
acteristics, and functions of MFs and PFs in ﬁbrotic scars. Meningeal
CE-F were located in the center of the ﬁbrotic scar, and perivascular/
pial LA-F in the edge of the ﬁbrotic scar. CE-F expressed higher levels of
genes related with cholesterol synthesis, and LA-F was involved in
cholesterol transport and angiogenesis. CE-F and LA-F also differed in
the expression of ECM related genes (Fig. 9).
Fig. 4 | Heterogeneous origin and spatial distribution of ﬁbroblasts in
ﬁbrotic scars. A Immunostaining for Lama1 (light blue) and Emb (green) at 14 dpi in
PDGFRβ-CreER::R26-TdT mice after transection SCI or crush SCI. TdT positive cells
are red. Dashed lines distinguish the lesion regions from normal tissues. The rec-
tangular boxes indicate the enlarged regions in the images below. Scale bar:
250 μm, 50 μm (enlarged view). B Immunostaining for Lama2 (blue light) and
Crabp2 (green) at 14 dpi in Col1a2-CreER::R26-TdT mice after transection SCI or
crush SCI. TdT positive cells are red. Dashed lines distinguish the lesion regions
from normal tissues. The rectangular boxes indicate the enlarged regions in the
images below. Scale bar: 250 μm, 50 μm (enlarged view). C Quantiﬁcation of
Lama1+ area index and Emb+ area index in transection SCI and crush SCI. Data are
shown as mean ± SEM. n = 3 mice per group. D Representative images showing the
expression pattern of TdT+ cells (red) consistent with that of Crabp2 (green) and
locate outside of Lama1+ signals (green) in uninjured Crabp2-CreER::R26-TdT mice.
Three repeats are performed independently. Dashed lines distinguish dura/ara-
chnoid from pia mater. Scale bar: 25 μm. E There is a low level of distribution of
Crabp2-TdT+ cells (red) in Lama1+ (green) exterior of ﬁbrotic scars after either
transection SCI or crush SCI. Dashed lines distinguish the lesion regions from
normal tissues. Three repeats are performed independently. Scale bar: 100 μm.
Source data are provided as a Source Data ﬁle.
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-50564-x
Nature Communications|   (2024) 15:6321 
9

A series of studies have used Glast-CreER mice in combination
with reporter mice to trace the fate of Glast+ cells following SCI and
found that a small proportion of PDGFRβ+ cells lining capillaries and
meninges, termed type A pericytes, contribute to stromal ﬁbroblasts
after either penetrating or non-penetrating SCI7,8,16 and traumatic brain
injury, ischemic stroke, multiple sclerosis, and glioblastoma16. How-
ever, a recent study demonstrated that the ﬁbrotic scar is derived
predominantly from meningeal ﬁbroblasts and perivascular ﬁbroblasts
after EAE using Col1a2-CreER mice crossed with reporter mice2.
Recently, scRNA-seq has been performed to investigate the molecular
deﬁnitions of blood vascular and vessel-associated cells in the adult
mouse brain17. This study revealed that Glast (Slc1a3) is expressed in
astrocytes,
pericytes,
and
ﬁbroblasts14,17.
We
utilized
Myh11-
CreER::R26-TdT and NG2-CreER::R26-TdT to trace pericytes/vSMCs.
Our results showed pericytes/vSMCs were minimally involved in the
formation of ﬁbrotic scar tissue.
To elucidate the identity of scar-forming ﬁbroblasts after SCI, we
performed scRNA-seq of PDGFRβ+ cells and lineage tracing with
Col1a2-CreER mice and Crabp2-CreER mice. Our data revealed that
the vast majority of scar cells after SCI are ﬁbroblasts. Furthermore,
regulation of vasculature development
regulation of metal ion transport
muscle system process
regulation of small GTPase mediated signal transduction
regulation of protein serine/threonine kinase activity
cell−substrate adhesion
Ras protein signal transduction
small GTPase mediated signal transduction
0
20
40
60
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-50564-x
Nature Communications|   (2024) 15:6321 
10

we also identiﬁed two types of ﬁbroblasts in the spinal cords of adult
mice, PFs and MFs, both of which contribute to ﬁbrotic scar forma-
tion with different spatial location. Of note, Glast+ cells, previously
thought of as type A pericytes7,8,16, are actually PFs. However, it is
worth to note that pericytes are a heterogeneous cell population51–53.
It cannot be completely ruled out that a subset of pericytes are
transformed into myoﬁbroblasts after spinal cord injury and lose
their pericyte properties. Meanwhile, Myh11+ and NG+ pericytes do
not represent the whole spinal cord pericytes. More in-depth studies
are needed to investigate heterogeneity of pericytes in the central
nervous system.
It has been reported that macrophage depletion leads to
attenuation of ﬁbrosis after SCI54, but the crosstalk between macro-
phages and ﬁbroblasts has remained unclear. Our data suggest that
microglia/macrophages secrete TGFB ligands that activate the TGF-β
signaling pathway in ﬁbroblasts and thereby promote ﬁbrosis. High
TGFB/TGFBR interaction scores were also present in pericytes/vSMCs
at 5 dpi. Previous studies have indicated that TGFB secreted by
endothelial cells binds to TGFBR2 in pericytes and activates down-
stream signaling pathways, thereby inhibiting pericyte proliferation
and inducing the expression of contractile protein and ECM proteins55.
The interaction score of PDGFB-PDGFRB was high in both endothelial
cells/ﬁbroblasts and endothelial cells/pericytes. PDGFB secreted by the
endothelial cells binds to PDGFRβ on pericytes and initiates a variety of
signal transduction pathways to regulate pericyte proliferation,
migration, and recruitment to the vascular wall56,57. In pericyte-speciﬁc
PDGFRβ signaling deﬁcient mice, pericyte loss leads to decreased
brain microcirculation and Blood-brain barrier (BBB) breakdown,
ultimately resulting in neuroinﬂammation and neurodegenerative
changes58.
Previous studies have shown that complete ablation of ﬁbrotic
scars is not conducive to damage repair, but partial reduction of scars
promotes axon regeneration and functional recovery7–13. In this study,
we found the heterogeneous functions of meningeal-derived CE-F and
perivascular/pial LA-F after SCI on lipid synthesis and lipid transport. It
suggests that speciﬁc ablation of subsets of ﬁbroblasts may differently
affect the injured microenvironment and functional recovery after SCI.
Methods
Transgenic mice and rhesus macaques
PDGFRβ-CreER::R26-TdT mice were generated by crossing PDGFRβ-
CreER mice59 (obtained from the Jackson Laboratory, Jax stock no.
030201) with the Rosa26-tdtomato cre reporter line (Jax stock no:
007909). CreER and R26-TdT mice resulting from this hybridization
were either homozygous or heterozygous. Similarly, NG2-CreER mice60
(obtained from the Jackson Laboratory, Jax stock no. 008538), Col1a2-
CreER mice61 (obtained from the Jackson Laboratory, Jax stock no.
029567), and Myh11-CreER mice62 (obtained from the Jackson
Laboratory, Jax stock no. 019079) were also crossed with the Rosa26-
tdtomato cre reporter line to generate NG2-CreER::R26-TdT mice,
Col1a2-CreER::R26-TdT mice, and Myh11-CreER::R26-TdT mice, which
are heterozygous for CreER and homozygous or heterozygous for R26-
TdT. We developed Crabp2-CreER mice by using CRISPR/Cas9 to insert
CreER fragments before Crabp2’s stop codon.
All mice were more than 8 weeks old at the beginning of the
experiments and included both males and females. Mice were housed
in a barrier system with a 12 h/12 h light/dark cycle, where temperature
and humidity were controlled within a range suitable for mice survival.
Water and food were sterilized and freely accessible to mice. Ten
female rhesus macaques (Macaca mulatta) aged 4–7 years old were
housed in Beijing Institute of Xieerxin Biology Resource. Experimental
procedures were performed according to Guide for the Care and Use of
Laboratory Animals which were formulated by the National Institutes
of Health (USA) and approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee
of the Institute of Genetics and Developmental Biology, Chinese
Academy of Sciences, and Use Committee of Beijing Institute of
Xieerxin Biology Resource. The approved animal protocol number of
monkeys is no. 20191017, and the approved animal protocol number of
mice is AP2019003 and AP2023031.
Tamoxifen treatment
One gram of tamoxifen (T5648, Sigma) was dissolved in 50 mL corn oil
(C116023, Aladdin) to make up a solution with a concentration of
20 mg/mL. Adult mice received intraperitoneal injection of tamoxifen
solution at 0.1 mg/g for 5 consecutive days.
BrdU injection
To label proliferating cells, mice were intraperitoneally administered
with BrdU (50 mg/kg of body weight) once a day for 13 consecutive
days from 1 dpi to 13 dpi. Mice were sacriﬁced at 14 dpi.
Surgical procedures
Mice were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of sodium pen-
tobarbital. Back fur was removed with a razor blade, and skin was
washed with povidone iodine solution. Then an incision in the skin and
a midline incision above the thoracic spine were made. A laminectomy
was performed to expose the T10 vertebra. For transection SCI
(penetrating), the exposed spinal cord was cut fully along the coronal
plane with microsurgical scissors. For crush SCI (non-penetrating), the
exposed spinal cords were completely crushed from two sides for 5 s
with forceps, with dura intact. After the introduction of both types of
SCI, the muscle layers and the skin were sutured successively. After
completing the surgery, mice were placed in a heated incubator until
fully awake from anesthesia. During the experiment, the bladders of
mice who underwent surgery were artiﬁcially emptied twice a day.
For Rhesus macaques, ketamine (1 mg/kg) was used for intramural
anesthesia, and 1.5%–2.5% isoﬂurane was used for anesthesia. The
physiological indexes of monkeys were monitored during the opera-
tion, namely oxygen saturation, cardiac rate, and respiratory fre-
quency, with venous transfusion of saline solution. The SCI procedures
were similar to those used for mice. Brieﬂy, an incision was made in the
skin and muscle, the T9 lamina was removed, and a cut was made
through the spinal membrane, thus exposing the spinal cord. Eight
millimeters of spinal cord tissue was removed to ensure complete

transection of the spinal cord. After successful hemostasis, the dura
was sutured, followed by the muscle and skin.
Tissue dissection and immunoﬂuorescence analysis
Mice were euthanized by intraperitoneal injection of excess
sodium
pentobarbital
and
then
transcardially
perfused
with
phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution for removing all the
blood, followed by perfusion with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA).
Spinal cords were dissected and immersed in 4% PFA overnight at
4 °C. PFA-ﬁxed tissues were then incubated with 30% sucrose
overnight at 4 °C, embedded in OCT (4583, SAKURA), and stored at
−80 °C. Coronal and horizontal sections were cut at 12–15 μm
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-50564-x

12

thickness using a cryostat (Leica), stuck to slides, and stored
at −80 °C.
Sections were incubated in blocking solution (10% donkey
serum and 0.3% Triton-X100) for 1 h at room temperature and then
incubated in primary antibody solution diluted with 10% donkey
serum (36116ES10, YEASEN) overnight at 4 °C. The primary anti-

Tissue dissociation and FACS
Mice. Ten male/ female PDGFRβ-CreER::R26-TdT mice per time points
(0 dpi, 5 dpi and 14 dpi) were euthanized by intraperitoneal injection of
excess sodium pentobarbital, and then transcardially perfused with
phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution to remove all the blood.
Spinal cord segments (4 mm) centered at the lesion core were isolated
and cut into small pieces with sharp scissors. The pieces were digested
with papain (LK003176, Worthington Biochemical) containing DNaseI
(LK003170, Worthington Biochemical) for 1 h at 37 °C with slow rota-
tion. Then 1.0 mg/mL collagenase type II (LS004176, Worthington
Biochemical) and 0.4 mg/mL neutral protease (LS02104, Worthington
Biochemical) were added, and the sample was incubated for 30 min at
at 37 °C with slow rotation. After centrifugation at 400×g for 5 min, the
cell pellets were resuspended with 25% Percoll (P1644, Sigma) and
centrifuged at 400×g for 10 min to remove myelin debris. Using a BD
FACSAria Fusion cell sorter, cells were sorted into DPBS buffer
according to tdtomato ﬂuorescence. The FSC/SSC was adjusted to
isolate single cells, then isolate target cells depending on the expres-
sion of tdtomato.
Rhesus macaques
Monkeys underwent transection SCI (8 mm) and were sacriﬁced at 7
dpi, 14 dpi, 30 dpi, and 6 mpi. Monkeys were euthanized by intra-
muscularly injection of ketamine. Single-nucleus RNA-seq was per-
formed on tissues dissected from the lesion edge, while scRNA-seq
was performed on tissues from the lesion core. To distinguish
between MFs and PFs in normal monkey spinal cord, we removed the
meninges of normal monkey prior to scRNA-seq. The method for
obtaining single cells was similar to that used for mice. Spinal cord
tissues dissociated from monkeys were digested into single cell
suspensions using a papain dissociation system (LK003150, Wor-
thington Biochemical)63. Debris was removed by Percoll density
gradient centrifugation. The method for obtaining single nuclei was
based on a published paper64. The nuclei were separated from the
cells using EZ Lysis Buffer (Sigma), and then cell debris were removed
by sucrose density gradient centrifugation. Tissue samples were
immersed in ice-cold Nuclei EZ lysis buffer (NUC101-1KT, Sigma) and
homogenized by using Dounce tissue grinder (D8938, Sigma). Grind
each sample 15 times with pestle A, then 15 times with pestle B. Dilute
the lysate with 0.32 M sucrose. After centrifugation at 3200 × g for
10 min and discard the supernatant, the precipitate was carefully re-
suspended with 0.32 M sucrose. The suspension was then lightly
covered with 1 M sucrose and centrifuged for 20 min at 3200×g. After
the supernatant was discarded, PBS was used to collect the nuclei
deposited on the tube wall. After centrifugation at 3200×g for
10 min, resuspend cell nuclei with PBS containing 0.01% BSA, and
0.1% RNase inhibitor (2313A, TaKaRa). All operations should be per-
formed on ice or at 2–8 °C.
Single-cell sequencing and analysis
One sample containing ten spinal cords was collected from PDGFRβ-
CreER::R26-TdT mice. Collected cells were passed through the 10×
Genomics pipeline (v3) and sequenced on an Illumina HiSeq 4000.
Low-quality cells with few genes expressed (<200) and a proportion of
mitochondrial genes <0.05 were removed. Several cell types in addi-
tion to ﬁbroblasts and pericytes were also observed by integrating data
from 0 dpi, 5 dpi, and 14 dpi samples, including astrocytes, oligoden-
drocytes, endothelial cells, ependymal cells, Schwann cells, myeloid
cells, and immune cells. To identify the origin of myoﬁbroblasts, we
Fig. 9 | Schematic summarization of heterogeneous contribution of PDGFRβ
cells and their progeny in ﬁbrotic scar after transection and crush SCI. A In the
intact spinal cord, there are two types of ﬁbroblasts, meningeal CE-F in dura and
arachnoid, and LA-F in the pia and parenchyma.Pericytes/vSMCs are mainly located
around blood vessels in spinal cord parenchyma. B After SCI, CE-F and LA-F migrate
into the lesion core and generate ECM. CE-F express higher levels of Col1 and
Fibronectin, and LA-F express higher levels of Col IV and Laminin. CE-F are active in
cholesterol synthesis, and LA-F are involved in lipid transportation/storage and
angiogenesis. CE-F and LA-F exhibit a speciﬁc spatial distribution within the ﬁbrotic
scar region, where LA-F are found on the lateral side of CE-F. Pericytes/vSMCs
contribute little to the ﬁbrotic scar formation.
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-50564-x
Nature Communications|   (2024) 15:6321 
15

captured clusters of ﬁbroblasts and pericytes/vSMCs depending on
cluster analysis and obtained a total of 33,366 cells. The sequencing
ﬁles were run through the 10× Cell Ranger 7.0.0 pipeline to generate
gene count data, and then Seurat (v3) and our customed R scripts were
applied for sequential analyses. The FindAllMarkers function was used
for gene differential expression analysis with the Wilcox method and
parameters min.pct 0.25 and logfc.threshold 0.25. Using a combina-
tion of classical marker expression and calculation of differential gene
expression between clusters, we obtained cell type information for all
samples.
Ligand–receptor interaction analysis. To explore cell-cell com-
munication and infer potential ligand–receptor interactions between
cell types, we applied CellPhoneDB65 on annotated Seurat single
sample datasets and multi-sample integrated objects. We downloaded
a list of all human and mouse orthologous pairs from the Ensembl
database. Before loading the gene expression matrix into CellPho-
neDB, we replaced mouse gene names with corresponding human
orthologous gene names. After obtaining the result ﬁles (means and P
values) of CellPhoneDB, we generated an R script to extract cell types
and ligand–receptor pairs of interest, then cell-cell communication
was visualized in a series of dot plots from ggplot2.
DEGs with a P value less than 0.05 and log-transformed fold
change larger than 0.25 were selected as input. The package cluster-
Proﬁler 3.18.1 and database org. Mm.eg.db 3.12.0 were used for GO
enrichment analysis. All three ontologies (biological process, cellular
component, and molecular function) were exported.
Isolation and culture of ﬁbroblasts
Two adult SD rats weighting about 250 g were sacriﬁced by intra-
peritoneal injection of excess sodium pentobarbital. Spinal cords
were dissected, then dura/arachnoid and pia were carefully removed
under a stereomicroscope. A cocktail of collagenase type I (C8140,
Solarbio) and II (LS004176, Worthington Biochemical) was used for
digestion of dura and pia. The dissociated cells were collected in a 15-
mL centrifuge tube after ﬁltered through a 100-mm nylon cell strai-
ner. Cell pellets were collected by centrifugation at 500×g for 5 min
and re-suspended in culture media containing DMEM (C11330,
GIBCO), 15% FBS (16000044, GIBCO), and penicillin/streptomycin
(15140122, GIBCO).
Myelin isolation
Two adult SD rats were sacriﬁced by intraperitoneal injection of excess
sodium pentobarbital. Take the telencephalon of two rats, discard the
cerebellum and brainstem, place them in a glass dish, and soak with
0.32 M sucrose. After peeling off the surface membrane and blood
vessels, cut them into small pieces and place them in a centrifuge tube,
and then use a 5 ml pipette to blow and shake. Add 6 mL 0.85 M
sucrose into each high-speed centrifuge tube, and then carefully add
2 mL tissue suspension to ensure a clear boundary between the two
solutions. After centrifugation at 27,000×rpm for 1 h, the protein was
located in the middle separation layer. Add pre cooled water to two
15 ml centrifuge tubes, remove the intermediate layer, place it in pre
cooled water, and leave it on ice for 30 min. Resuspending the pellet
and store at −80 °C after centrifugation at 12,000×g for 30 min. All
steps are performed on ice or at 4 °C.
RT–qPCR
Total RNA of ﬁbroblasts cultured in vitro was extracted using an
Ultrapure RNA Kit (CW0581S, CWBIO). Reverse transcription was
performed by a Hifair® II 1st Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit (11123ES,
YEASEN). RT-qPCR was performed with Hieff® qPCR SYBR® Green
Master Mix (11201ES, YEASEN) on a real-time PCR instrument (Bio-
RAD). The list of primers used is shown in Table 1.
Endothelial cell tube formation
Matrigel (354230, Corning) was brieﬂy placed in the lower chambers of
a 12-well transwell plate (1 mL/well) and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C.
Endothelial cells (SCC066, Sigma) were then seeded at a concentration
of 5 × 105/mL (800 μL/chamber). PFs or MFs at a density of 1 × 105/mL
were cultured in ﬁbroblast culture medium and seeded on the upper
chambers (500 μL/chamber). Images were captured to examine the
blood vessel formation after 12-h co-culture.
Quantiﬁcation and statistical analysis
To characterize TdT+ cells in the uninjured and injured spinal cord of
PDGFRβ-CreER::R26-TdT mice, tissue sections from uninjured spinal
cord were stained with PDGFRβ, Sox9, Sox10 and Pdx. Tissue sec-
tions from injured spinal cord were stained with Desmin, CD31,
CD68, Sox9 and Sox10. To calculate the number of TdT+ cells in the
lesion core, tissue sections from PDGFRβ-CreER::R26-TdT mice at
different time points in the crush and transection SCI models were
stained for GFAP, which marked the astrocyte scar border between
the lesion core and spared tissue. The number of TdT+ cells in the
GFAP-negative area was counted using Adobe Photoshop, while the
area of the corresponding section was measured using ImageJ soft-
ware. To quantify the percentage of dividing cells or Nestin+ cells
among TdT+ cells in the lesion core, tissue sections from PDGFRβ-
CreER::R26-TdT mice subjected to crush SCI or transection SCI were
stained for Ki67 or Nestin at 5 dpi. Cell nuclei were stained by incu-
bating with DAPI.
To characterize TdT+ cells in the uninjured Col1a2-CreER::R26-
TdT mice, tissue sections from uninjured spinal cord were stained with
PDGFRβ and Col1.
Tissue sections from uninjured Myh11-CreER::R26-TdT mice were
stained with CD13, Myh11, Desmin and αSMA. For NG2-CreER::R26-TdT

All data are reported as mean ± SEM, and n values represent the

using GraphPad Prism 8.

===== Younesi2024_NatRevMolCellBiol_Fibroblast+Myofibroblast =====
Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
617
nature reviews molecular cell biology
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41580-024-00716-0
Review article
 Check for updates
Fibroblast and myofibroblast 
activation in normal tissue 
repair and fibrosis
Fereshteh Sadat Younesi1,2,5, Andrew E. Miller 
  3,5, Thomas H. Barker 
  3, Fabio M. V. Rossi 
  4 & Boris Hinz 
  1,2 
Abstract
The term ‘fibroblast’ often serves as a catch-all for a diverse array 
of mesenchymal cells, including perivascular cells, stromal progenitor 
cells and bona fide fibroblasts. Although phenotypically similar, 
these subpopulations are functionally distinct, maintaining tissue 
integrity and serving as local progenitor reservoirs. In response to 
tissue injury, these cells undergo a dynamic fibroblast–myofibroblast 
transition, marked by extracellular matrix secretion and contraction 
of actomyosin-based stress fibres. Importantly, whereas transient 
activation into myofibroblasts aids in tissue repair, persistent activation 
triggers pathological fibrosis. In this Review, we discuss the roles 
of mechanical cues, such as tissue stiffness and strain, alongside cell 
signalling pathways and extracellular matrix ligands in modulating 
myofibroblast activation and survival. We also highlight the role 
of epigenetic modifications and myofibroblast memory in physiological 
and pathological processes. Finally, we discuss potential strategies 
for therapeutically interfering with these factors and the associated 
signal transduction pathways to improve the outcome of dysregulated 
healing.
Sections
Introduction
Fibroblast functions and 
phenotypes
Myofibroblast activation
Mechanisms of (myo-)
fibroblast mechanoperception
Mechanisms of myofibroblast 
mechanotransduction
Myofibroblast fates and 
memory
Conclusions and perspectives
1Keenan Research Institute for Biomedical Science of the St. Michael’s Hospital, Toronto, Ontario, Canada. 
2Faculty of Dentistry, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, Canada. 3Department of Biomedical Engineering, 
School of Engineering and Applied Science, University of Virginia, Charlottesville, VA, USA. 4School of Biomedical 
Engineering and Department of Medical Genetics, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, British Columbia, 
Canada. 5These authors contributed equally: Fereshteh Sadat Younesi, Andrew E. Miller. 
 e-mail: boris.hinz@
utoronto.ca

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
618
Review article
the mechanical (tissue stiffness and strain) and biochemical (growth 
factors and ECM ligands) environment in promoting fibroblastic cell 
activation, function and survival. We present how continued exposure 
to these factors creates epigenetic memory that turns transient myofi-
broblasts into persistent myofibroblasts. Different concepts of thera-
peutic interference with the respective fibroblast-specific signalling 
recognition and transduction mechanisms are presented, with the aim 
of devising strategies to improve the outcomes of dysregulated healing.
Fibroblast functions and phenotypes
We now understand that multiple different cell types, including fibro-
blasts, can become activated into myofibroblasts after acute tissue 
injury, which is a particularly efficient strategy used by the body to 
rapidly repair structural damage10 (Fig. 1a), irrespective of where in 
the body the damage occurs. The vast majority of myofibroblast pre-
cursors are of mesenchymal origin although some reports suggest 
that epithelial cells23,24 (through epithelial–mesenchymal transition), 
vascular endothelial cells (by endothelial–mesenchymal transition)25, 
and circulating CD45-positive cells of the haematopoietic lineage, such 
as fibrocytes and macrophages26,27, can contribute to the myofibro-
blast pool under specific conditions and in a tissue-specific manner. 
For instance, in the central nervous system, astrocytes and glial cells, 
in addition to pericytes, can attain the myofibroblast phenotype and 
function to produce collagen scars17,28. The most common mesenchy-
mal myofibroblast precursors include adipocytes, pericytes, smooth 
muscle cells (SMCs), multipotent mesenchymal stromal or stem cells 
(MSCs)29–31, and bona fide fibroblasts19,29,32,33. In the following section, 
we will try to untangle some of the confusing fibroblast–myofibroblast 
nomenclature. It should be emphasized that the myofibroblast is a 
phenotype that many different precursors can acquire to perform 
repair functions and not a distinct cell type.
Fibroblast and myofibroblast heterogeneity
That fibroblasts are a vastly heterogeneous group has long been recog-
nized, even in the pre-single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) era34. If 
anything, one commonality of fibroblastic cells is the difficulty to define 
unique molecular markers — hence their original denomination as fibro-
plastic3. There seems to be general agreement that fibroblastic cells in 
most normal tissues (that is, not activated) express collagen type I35 and 
the platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) receptor-α (PDGFRα)36. Other 
frequently used fibroblast markers are vimentin, hypermethylated in 
cancer 1 (Hic1)37,38, CD90 (Thy1)39, transcription factor 21 (TCF21)30 and 
fibroblast-specific protein 1 (FSP1; also known as S100A4)40. However, 
many of these mesenchymal markers are also expressed in SMCs and 
pericytes and are thus not specific to fibroblasts. In contrast to fibro-
blasts, pericytes and SMCs both express desmin, and (non-activated) 
pericytes are positive for chondroitin sulfate proteoglycan 4 (NG2) and 
PDGFRβ but not PDGFRα41–43. Exclusion criteria (for example, being 
positive for mesenchymal vimentin, but negative for both the muscle 
marker desmin and the endothelial cell marker CD31) can be employed 
to discriminate fibroblastic cells from other common tissue cell types 
using conventional immunohistochemistry44 (Box 1).
Another prominent group of mesenchymal myofibroblast pre-
cursors easily confounded with fibroblasts are MSCs, where even 
the letters in the acronym are a matter of debate, with ‘S’ standing for 
‘stromal’ or ‘stem’, and ‘M’ meaning mesenchymal or multipotent45–47. 
MSCs together with fibro-adipogenic progenitors and pericytes often 
reside in a perivascular niche29. With their long projections extend-
ing along vessels, perivascular cells are ideally positioned to sense 
Introduction
The term ‘fibroblast’ generically describes a connective tissue cell 
with the ability to produce connective tissue substance (collagen). 
This minimal definition is attributed to Rudolph Virchow, who, in 
1858, described distinct spindle-shaped cells embedded in fibrous 
connective tissue1. However, Theodor Schwann had already identi-
fied spindle-shaped cellular elements within connective tissue when 
he defined cells as the basic unit of the animal body in 1839 (ref. 2). In 
1848, Hermann Liebert called these cellular elements “geschwänzte 
fibroplastische Körperchen”, which rather cutely translates to ‘tailed 
fibroplastic corpuscles’3. Ernst Ziegler then coined the term ‘fibroblast’ 
when summarizing the content of wound granulation tissue in 1885: “In 
most cases the hypertrophied cells are connective-tissue cells, which 
later produce connective tissue […], and may therefore be termed 
fibroblasts”4. Although this cell type has at least 180 years of research 
history since its discovery, ‘fibroblasts’, in fact, comprise a vastly hetero- 
geneous group of mesenchymal cells with similar morphology but 
specific functions in maintaining tissue extracellular matrix (ECM) 
homeostasis and regulating local biological processes in the healthy 
adult organism. However, in conditions of tissue injury and in the pres-
ence of danger signals, such as following ECM disruption or inflam-
mation, they suspend their specialized functions and acquire repair 
features aimed at rapidly containing the tissue damage by producing 
fibrous collagen-rich ECM and eventually contracting the newly formed 
ECM into mechanically stabilizing scar tissue. This phenotypic switch 
is called fibroblast–myofibroblast activation (in short: myofibroblast 
activation)5,6.
The concept that wounds are closed by an active cell component 
rather than by autonomously ‘condensing’ wound collagen was intro-
duced as a dogma shift in the 1950s and changed the understanding of 
tissue repair7. These ground-breaking seminal studies by Abercrom-
bie et al. paved the way for the discovery of the myofibroblast in the 
early 1970s by Gabbiani et al. in transmission electron micrographs 
of wound granulation tissue of healing injuries in rats8. The authors 
described wound fibroblasts that exhibited a “fibrillar system devel-
oped within the cytoplasm”, which “were no longer the few fibrils often 
seen in fibroblasts, but bundles of packed fibrils resembling those of 
smooth muscle”8. In a subsequent functional study, the same authors 
concluded that “fibroblasts, under certain conditions, are capable of 
modulating towards a cell type that is structurally and functionally 
close to smooth muscle; for these cells the name ‘myofibroblast’ may 
be appropriate”9. In this Review, we provide and revisit definitions of 
different cells with a fibroblast phenotype in the adult organism in their 
normal and active state, one of which is the myofibroblast.
Transient activation of fibroblastic cells into myofibroblasts is 
crucial and beneficial to repair acute tissue damage whereas their per-
sistent activation characterizes and drives organ scarring, a debilitating 
and often lethal condition called fibrosis10 (Fig. 1a). Why myofibroblasts 
persist in fibrosis but disappear following acute repair is not entirely 
clear, and solving this conundrum is considered the holy grail in fibrosis 
research. For deeper insight into the specific contributions of myofi-
broblasts in pathological settings, the reader is referred to excellent 
recent reviews on the fibrosis of different organs and tissues, including 
the heart11,12, kidneys13, liver14, intestine15,16, central nervous system17, 
lungs18, skin10,19, palmar fascia (that is, the triangular connective tissue 
structure in the palm of the hand)20 and eyes21,22. In addition to their 
molecular identity, the local environment is crucial to determining 
the function and fate of fibroblastic cells in physiological and patho-
logical tissue repair. Here, we will mainly elaborate on the impact of

Fig. 1 | Myofibroblast activation states in normal and pathological tissue 
repair. This figure illustrates the different stages of the tissue repair process 
and the involvement of different cell types after an injury to the skin but, in 
principle, these processes apply to any organ injury. a, During haemostasis, 
bleeding is stopped by a platelet-initiated fibrin clot that also serves as a scaffold 
for growth factors and cells that populate the early wound bed. This is followed 
by inflammation involving early-responding mast cells, neutrophils and 
inflammatory macrophages that fight infections and phagocytose debris  
and secrete factors that coordinate the healing process. The proliferation phase 
is characterized by neovascularization, recruitment of repair macrophages and 
activation of resident cells in the adjacent normal tissue to divide and migrate 
into the wound. Organs with epithelium are re-epithelialized and fibroblastic 
cells migrate into the provisional extracellular matrix (ECM) to produce collagen, 
which is further aligned and organized into scar tissue in the remodelling phase. 
Resolving macrophages contribute to the volume reduction of the wound bed by 
removing excess ECM. In physiological repair, macrophages and myofibroblasts 
are eventually cleared by apoptosis, when the original structure and function of 
normal tissue are largely restored, allowing scarless healing. In fibrotic repair, 
contractile myofibroblasts and repair macrophages persist and continue to drive 
scar ECM production and stiffening, leading to hypertrophic scars.  
b, Fibroblast activation into contractile myofibroblasts is tightly controlled by 
the organization state (that is, the biophysical properties) of the wound ECM and 
vice versa. Starting in the inflammatory phase, fibroblastic cells are activated 
from a homeostatic ‘quiescent’ progenitor state to proliferate and migrate into 
the provisional fibrin-rich ECM. The low traction forces of migrating fibroblasts 
initially result in the alignment and later in the condensation of fibrin fibrils. 
Fibrin-rich ECM is progressively replaced by fibrous collagens produced by 
initially non-contractile matrix fibroblasts in the proliferation phase. Once 
the denser and stiffer collagen ECM becomes too resistant to low fibroblast 
traction forces, it stimulates fibroblast-mediated formation of contractile 
actomyosin bundles (so-called stress fibres) in later wound stages. The higher 
contractile forces of such (proto-)myofibroblasts produce even stiffer scar 
ECM that can withstand external forces and restores the mechanical integrity 
of the organ. To match this higher mechanical resistance, myofibroblasts 
start to express α-smooth muscle actin (αSMA), the incorporation of which 
into stress fibres hallmarks the highly contractile myofibroblast. Along with 
enhanced extracellular and intracellular stress, cell–ECM adhesions develop 
from so-called immature focal complexes into mature focal adhesions and then 
into the supermature focal adhesions typical for αSMA-positive myofibroblasts. 
Maturation refers to the gradually increasing size and molecular complexity of 
myofibroblast adhesion sites. As a consequence, myofibroblasts generate the 
mechanical conditions that stimulate and maintain their contractile phenotype 
in a positive feedback loop. Stress fibre-rich, αSMA-expressing myofibroblasts 
were formerly referred to as differentiated; however, this may not be accurate 
given that they can revert into stress fibre-negative cells after withdrawal of 
mechanical stress and pro-fibrotic signalling factors. Therefore, the distinction 
should instead be made between persistent myofibroblasts that prevail in organ 
fibrosis and transient myofibroblasts that are cleared by apoptosis during 
physiological repair. Identifying the mechanisms that discriminate one from the 
other is among the biggest challenges in fibrosis research.

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638

Review article
environmental changes and are among the first responders to tissue 
damage and danger signals29. Because these cells also reside in the 
adventitial connective tissue layer of larger vessels they are some-
times called fibroblasts. However, multipotent perivascular ‘mural 
cells’ should be treated distinctly from adventitial fibroblasts as the 
latter lack progenitor potential, that is, the ability to differentiate into 
multiple different cell types48–50. As for specific markers, plasticity is a 
virtue of MSCs47. To name an example, fibro-adipogenic progenitors 
are considered MSCs because they can differentiate into (at least) 
fibroblasts and adipocytes in skeletal and heart muscle; in these tis-
sues, they express characteristic markers such as PDGFRα, Sca1, Hic1 
and Gli1 (refs. 37,51,52).
Fibroblastic progenitor cell populations with similar proposed 
functions have been described for most organs, and aligning their anno-
tation with molecular marker sets across organs is ongoing50,53 (Box 1). 
For instance, different fibroblast subpopulations have been charac-
terized in the skin according to their location in the adipose, facial, 
reticular and papillary layers, function, and characteristic protein and 
transcription profiles38,54. Likewise, lung fibroblasts have been classified 
as airway-associated alveolar fibroblasts, lipofibroblasts and matrix 
fibroblasts with distinct predicted functions and locations55–59. At steady 
state, that is, in normal (mouse) tissues, careful curation of publicly 
available and experimentally generated scRNA-seq datasets identified 
common gene signatures of PDGFRα-sorted fibroblasts in all organs, 
including the expression of Pi16 (encoding peptidase inhibitor 16), 
 Col15a1 (encoding collagen α1(XV) chain) and Dpt (encoding dermato-
pontin)53. In contrast to other tissue-specific fibroblast-associated 
genes (Box 1), Dpt emerges as a ‘universal’ tissue fibroblast marker, and 
its expression level is inversely correlated with fibroblast specialization 
for tissue-specific functions53. Dpt-positive fibroblasts also give rise to 
Lrrc15-positive (encoding leucine-rich repeat-containing 15 (LRRC15)) 
fibroblasts, which are prominent myofibroblast precursors in different 
injury conditions, including cancer53,60.
One potential confounder of scRNA-seq data is that distinct clus-
ters in uniform manifold approximation and projection or t-distributed 
stochastic neighbour embedding plots (Box 2) are interpreted as 
Box 1
Myofibroblast detection and characterization
For the diagnosis and grading of fibroproliferative diseases, it is 
important to identify myofibroblasts in histological sections of 
biopsies, but how myofibroblasts are best characterized in pathology 
and research applications remains an important question. Bundles 
of actin microfilaments, although sufficient for (proto-)myofibroblast 
identification, cannot be specifically detected in standard histological 
procedures because the filamentous actin-binding probe phalloidin 
is incompatible with the organic solvents used in the process44. 
Detection of α-smooth muscle actin (αSMA) with actin isoform-
specific antibodies is the most widely used method to identify 
myofibroblasts in cell culture and sections of fibroproliferative 
tissues306. Other markers that are characteristic of the myofibroblast 
cytoskeleton in various fibrotic organs and conditions are the 
transmembrane receptors α11β1 integrin307, cadherin 11 (also 
known as OB-cadherin)305,308,309, platelet-derived growth factor 
receptor-β (PDGFRβ)42,153 and, possibly, leucine-rich repeat-
containing 15 (LRRC15)53. Extracellular matrix proteins associated 
with myofibroblasts include extradomain A (ED-A) fibronectin133, 
periostin310, tenascin C311, latent transforming growth factor-β-binding 
protein 1 (LTBP1)155,163, CCN2 (formerly called connective tissue growth 
factor; CTGF)312 and, determined only recently, the protein collagen 
triple helix repeat-containing 1 (CTHRC1)56,313. Proteomic and spatial 
proteomic approaches will likely expand the list of extracellular 
matrix-associated myofibroblast markers314,315. The expression levels 
and thus usefulness of these proteins as myofibroblast markers 
vary depending on tissue and condition. All these frequently used 
markers, including αSMA-positive stress fibres, are upregulated 
during myofibroblast activation from various precursors but are 
not exclusive to myofibroblasts. For instance, αSMA is expressed in 
contractile bundles of smooth muscle cells (SMCs) and myoepithelial 
cells and as a cytoplasmic protein (that is, not in stress fibres) in 
pericytes316,317. ED-A fibronectin is also expressed by vascular SMCs165 
and cadherin 11 is upregulated in fibroblast-like synoviocytes in 
rheumatoid diseases318, that is, it is also characteristic of immune 
fibroblasts119.
Combinatorial assessments with exclusion markers are often 
required to discriminate myofibroblasts from phenotypically related 
cells that occupy similar spaces in relevant tissues. SMCs, as one of 
the most prominent confounders, express specific muscle markers 
such as desmin, smoothelin and smooth muscle myosin that are 
absent from myofibroblasts44. Other proteins that are typically 
not expressed by myofibroblasts but are by their non-activated 
precursors are PDGFRα36 and, in specific conditions, Thy1 membrane 
glycoprotein. The latter has a functional role in regulating mechanical 
fibroblast–myofibroblast initial activation but was reported to be 
absent from later myofibroblast activation stages319. Whether Thy1 
is indicative of myofibroblasts (for example, in scleroderma skin)320 
or absent from myofibroblasts in fibrotic lesions (for example, lungs 
and heart)39,321 seems to vary across different fibrotic organs. In 
cancer-associated fibroblasts, high expression of Thy1 is indicative of 
cancer progression in contrast to Thy1-negative cancer-associated 
fibroblasts, which indicate earlier stages of cancer development322. 
Understanding of the role of Thy1 is further confounded by the 
fact that mRNA signatures do not necessarily inform functional 
phenotype. Thy1 mRNA signatures are often diffusely spread 
across fibroblast subpopulations annotated from single-cell RNA 
sequencing datasets. However, the expression or lack of Thy1 on the 
surface of fibroblastic cells consistently yields discrete functional 
phenotypes that are clearly not captured in uniform manifold 
approximation and projection or t-distributed stochastic neighbour 
embedding plots of single-cell RNA sequencing but are rather due 
to the molecular function of Thy1 protein. In fact, Thy1 and other 
proteins whose transcriptional states do not align with annotated 
fibroblast subpopulations but confer clear functional phenotypes 
may present a new axis in the further discrimination of fibroblast and 
myofibroblast functions.

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
621
Review article
differentiated fibroblast types but may merely reflect the transcrip-
tional profiles related to their current function — that is, they may not be 
terminally differentiated. In the case of fully differentiated fibroblasts, 
transition from one into another fibroblast type should not be possible. 
However, MSCs and fibroblastic cells have the amazing capacity to 
adapt phenotypically, functionally and transcriptionally — not only 
to the different environments that exist across tissues but also to the 
niches within the same normal adult organs50. It exceeds the scope of 
this Review to list all the different fibroblast states or types with their 
respective marker sets and the reader is referred to reviews covering 
fibroblast heterogeneity specifically in the lungs61, skin33, heart62, 
kidneys13, liver63 and intestine15.
Most of these populations can become activated into myofibro-
blasts, yet with different quantitative contributions to the overall 
myofibroblast pool depending on their location and the severity and 
chronicity of organ injury. One possible exception is so-called immune 
or inflammatory fibroblasts that seem to be activated from the same 
perivascular precursor cells after tissue damage, like myofibroblasts64, 
but then follow a different path (Box 3). Fibroblastic cells with immune 
cell functions also exist in normal tissues — for example, the special-
ized fibroblastic reticular cells that support immune B and T cells in 
lymphoid organs65,66, and the fibroblast-like synoviocytes that line the 
joints67. The proposed dichotomy between fibroblastic cells with either 
immune (cytokine secretion) or myofibroblast (ECM secretion and 
contraction) functions is best studied for cancer-associated fibroblasts 
that form and populate the scar-like stroma around tumours68,69. Recent 
non-peer-reviewed computational predictions suggest that inflamma-
tory fibroblasts could be an intermediate precursor to ECM-producing 
fibroblasts in mouse and human lungs70. Likewise, CD201-positive 
progenitor cells in subcutaneous fascia were shown to contribute to 
mouse dermal wound healing by passing through a ‘pro-inflammatory’ 
state before acquiring myofibroblast features and functions71. Thus, 
whether immune fibroblasts and myofibroblasts are sequential or 
distinct states remains an open question. We will next consider the 
myofibroblast activation state responsible for the functional aspects 
of tissue repair.
Myofibroblasts
The ability to exert contractile force is, quite literally, part of the myofi-
broblast identity and the functional definition of these cells. Cytoskel-
etal actin–myosin bundles in vivo are the structural prerequisite for 
contractile function and the defining morphological feature of myofi-
broblasts (Fig. 1b and Box 1). Simply put, cells that do not contract or 
are missing contractile cytoskeletal elements cannot be and should 
not be labelled myofibroblasts72. This is an important consideration, as 
fibroblastic cells will adopt different functions during different stages 
of tissue repair, such as enhanced proliferation, migration, cytokine and 
ECM secretion, ECM remodelling, and phagocytosis10 (Fig. 1b). Because 
these functions and the related transcriptional profiles are acquired 
de novo after injury, the underlying process is typically referred to as 
‘fibroblast activation’. All these different fibroblast activation states are 
critical for proper tissue repair but alone do not meet the myofibroblast 
criterion unless contractile features and function are present.
In standard cell culture conditions, that is, on plastic dishes in the 
presence of serum, all cultured fibroblastic cells form actin–myosin 
bundles, called stress fibres in vitro, and are therefore technically myofi-
broblasts. To emphasize this fact, we previously introduced the term 
‘proto-myofibroblast’ as a pre-activation state of highly contractile 
(at the time called ‘differentiated’) myofibroblasts, and both of these 
activation states coexist in cell cultures5. The stress fibres of highly con-
tractile myofibroblasts are distinct from those of proto-myofibroblasts 
by additionally containing the actin isoform α-smooth muscle actin 
(αSMA)73 (Fig. 1b). In vivo formation of actin filament bundles precedes 
and is likely a pre-condition for the stress fibre incorporation of αSMA 
during skin wound healing74. Incorporation of αSMA into pre-existing 
β-cytoplasmic actin filament bundles enhances the contractile activity 
of stress fibres73, which can be abolished by pharmacological removal of 
αSMA from stress fibres using competitive peptides75. It is still unclear 
how αSMA confers higher contractile activity than other actin isoforms 
at similar expression levels and how αSMA recruitment to stress fibres 
is controlled at the molecular level. It is conceivable that the contribu-
tion of specific tropomyosin isoforms76 and/or actin isoform-specific 
filament nucleation factors of the formin family77 is responsible for the 
differing functions. It remains to be shown if some of these ‘accessory’ 
Box 2
Single-cell approaches to the 
study of fibroblasts
Over the past years, single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has 
become a powerful tool with the potential to transform fibroblast 
research and enhance understanding of lineage relationships 
between these enigmatic cells. When interpreting uniform manifold 
approximation and projection or t-distributed stochastic neighbour 
embedding plots of scRNA-seq data we need to be aware that 
clusters are typically produced in an unbiased fashion based on 
gene expression similarities whereas the annotation of the clusters 
is not unbiased. For example, the widely used public databases 
CellMarker 2.0 and CancerSEA323,324 are manually curated and rely 
on the accuracy of studies that contributed to the formulation of 
fibroblast definition criteria. Even unbiased annotation creates 
self-reinforcing biases based on what is known, or believed to be 
known, from pre-existing literature and the specific interpretation 
of the literature by those defining what constitutes distinct and 
different subpopulations. Users of such databases should be 
aware that genes such as ACTA2 (encoding α-smooth muscle actin 
(αSMA)) and DES (encoding desmin) appear high in fibroblast 
annotation lists but are hardly suitable fibroblast markers, since 
they are not normally expressed in fibroblasts. Likewise, DES and 
MYH11 (encoding smooth muscle myosin heavy chain) are listed 
as myofibroblast markers, but they are not typically expressed in 
myofibroblasts. Other confounding factors of scRNA-seq datasets 
are inconsistent isolation procedures of fibroblastic cells: as 
these cells are usually embedded in dense connective tissues, 
enrichment of fibroblasts for subsequent analysis often uses the 
expression of platelet-derived growth factor receptor-α (PDGFRα)53 
or type I collagen as sorting criteria56. Such inclusion and exclusion 
criteria will necessarily result in bias, although experimental or 
computational cell sorting are currently required to manage 
clustering algorithms that demand significant computational 
resources. As technology progresses, such biases will become less 
of an issue.

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
622
Review article
proteins bear value as myofibroblast molecular markers as suggested 
for palladin, a protein that is expressed in different isoforms, some of 
which are specifically found in myofibroblast stress fibres78,79. Other 
criteria to characterize myofibroblasts and discriminate them from 
other cell types are summarized in Box 1.
Proliferation, migration, ECM production, formation of contrac-
tile stress fibres, expression of αSMA and incorporation of αSMA into 
existing stress fibres are generally viewed as sequential but overlap-
ping features of individual fibroblastic cells (Fig. 1b). However, it is 
also conceivable that different fibroblast precursor subpopulations 
(or lineages) specialize differently according to the tissue repair con-
text, like the immune fibroblasts discussed above and in Box 3, and as 
described for different subpopulations of dermal and fascial fibro-
blasts during skin wound healing80. Furthermore, it is plausible that 
fibroblast functional specialization occurs at the expense of versatility 
and that highly contractile myofibroblasts may no longer be proficient 
at ECM secretion (or never were because they did not pass through an 
ECM-producing state). Distinct fibroblast lineages and/or specializa-
tion stages can explain the separation of ‘matrix fibroblasts’ (a tautol-
ogy, really, because fibroblasts are ECM-producing cells and thus by 
definition reside in the matrix) and contractile myofibroblasts, for 
instance, in scRNA-seq clusters generated from fibroblast profiles in 
developing and fibrotic lungs56,81,82. Although not consistently tested 
in experimental models, it is conceivable that upregulation of con-
tractile activity negatively feeds back on ECM production, and vice 
versa. After all, contraction is only meaningful if there is a substrate 
to contract. Functional specialization can also explain why lung fibro-
blasts experimentally sorted for high collagen promoter activity and 
Box 3
Immune functions of fibroblasts
Fibroblastic cells are increasingly shown to acquire bona fide 
immune functions in response to injury and danger signals, 
such as loss of extracellular matrix (ECM) and vascular integrity 
and inflammation. Whether such immune fibroblasts represent 
a distinct fibroblast type that is mutually exclusive with ECM-
producing and contracting functions of myofibroblasts, or whether 
both functional states can transition into each other, is a matter 
of ongoing debate. Confirming earlier reports of IL-1-activated 
immune cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) and transforming 
growth factor-β1 (TGFβ1)-activated myofibroblastic ‘myCAFs’325, 
these distinct CAF subpopulations have recently been expanded by 
single-cell RNA sequencing-based pseudotime lineage tracing in 
pancreatic cancer326. The study supported the notion that TGFβ1-
driven, leucine-rich repeat-containing 15 (LRRC15)-positive CAFs with 
structural support functions and IL-1-driven LRRC15-negative CAFs 
with immunoregulatory functions derive from two distinct ‘resting’ 
fibroblasts populations. However, a later study suggested a shared 
trajectory with a common, Pi16-positive root precursor state that 
becomes activated via Col15a1-positive intermediates53.
The mechanisms that mediate the ability of fibroblastic cells 
to detect and communicate tissue damage overlap the molecular 
damage sensors used by innate immune cells. For instance, 
fibroblasts and professional immune cells both rely on the same 
families of danger signal receptors, including Toll-like receptors 
(TLRs), intracellular nucleotide-binding oligomerization domain 
containing 1, IL-1 receptor (IL-1R) and receptors for advanced glycation 
end products119. Advanced glycation end products are the products 
of non-enzymatic reactions between reducing sugars with proteins, 
lipids or nucleic acids that accumulate with age and other fibrosis 
risk factors such as diabetes. These commonalities suggest that 
some fibroblasts, like inflammatory cells, can directly sense damage-
associated molecular patterns (DAMPs). DAMPs released from 
necrotic myocardial cells in the heart were shown to directly activate 
fibroblasts in vitro327. In vivo, however, it has been difficult to assess 
whether the various DAMPs and alarmins released after tissue damage 
act directly on fibroblasts or do so, for example, by first triggering 
inflammatory cells to produce cytokines that activate fibroblasts.
There is strong evidence of fibroblastic cell activation, including 
cytokine expression, in the absence of inflammatory infiltrates 
from central nervous system injury models of denervation, as well 
as in patients suffering from amyotrophic lateral sclerosis328. That 
fibroblasts may indeed be the first responders to tissue damage and 
initiate inflammation is further supported by the fact that cytokine 
production, including of chemotactic factors for inflammatory cells, 
is among their initial functions following specification. Following 
tissue injury, fibroblastic cells exhibit highly upregulated levels 
of chemokines known to recruit type 1, early response immune 
cells from the circulation such as C-C motif chemokine 2 (CCL2; 
also known as monocyte chemoattractant protein 1), which is a 
main signal for monocyte extravasation329, C-X-C motif chemokine 
ligand 14, which amplifies chemotactic responses, and CCL5, 
an attractant for neutrophils330. Fibroblastic cells also release 
cytokines associated with type 2 responses, such as CCL11, an 
eosinophil-attracting chemokine, and IL-33, supporting type 2 innate 
lymphoid cells and T regulatory cells331,332. The recruited and tissue-
resident inflammatory cells then produce multiple cytokines that 
can, in turn, act on fibroblasts, including TGFβ, IL-4, IL-13, hepatocyte 
growth factor (HGF), IGF1 and IL-1 (ref. 287).
The IL-1R signalling pathway is likely to have a central role in 
this circuit between immune fibroblasts and inflammatory cells. 
Ubiquitous deletion of IL-1R leads to a reduction in both inflammatory 
cells and cytokines, as well as myofibroblast formation and ECM 
deposition in the ischaemic heart333. IL-1α treatment of isolated 
cardiac fibroblasts in vitro directly induces the expression of 
inflammatory cytokines, such as tumour necrosis factor (TNF), IL-6 
and IL-1β334, all of which act on inflammatory cells, suggesting that 
IL-1α elicits a fibroblast-driven positive feedback on inflammatory 
cell function. Thus, a fibroblast-initiated self-reinforcing circuit with 
inflammatory cells seems to be established following tissue damage, 
where the full inflammatory response is only achieved when both 
cell systems are engaged by DAMPs, potentially providing multiple 
layers of control against inappropriate activation of a regenerative 
environment that fosters tissue repair.

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
623
Review article
expression exhibit no or only low levels of the contractile marker 
αSMA56. It remains to be shown whether these populations have con-
tractile capacity by forming αSMA-negative stress fibres. However, this 
question cannot be answered by assessing mRNA or using standard 
(solvent-based) histological processing alone (Box 2). Whether the 
proposed functional specialization states of fibroblasts are transient 
(as in normal repair) or more permanent (as has been suggested for 
fibrosis) will be discussed in our later section on myofibroblast fates.
Myofibroblast activation
The mechanisms of myofibroblast activation appear remarkably similar 
across different pathophysiological conditions of tissue injury and 
repair despite the heterogeneity of their precursor cells, as discussed 
above. In the following sections, we focus on mechanical factors 
because the contraction of fibrous collagen ECM by myofibroblasts 
results in tissue stiffening, which in turn enhances myofibroblast activa-
tion. Breaking this positive feedback loop is one of the most challeng-
ing, but also most promising, strategies for targeting myofibroblasts 
in anti-fibrotic or pro-healing therapies. We will first describe key bio-
chemical signals, that is, cytokines, growth factors and ECM-associated 
molecules (Fig. 2), which are also pursued as anti-fibrotic targets and 
are crucial for the understanding of subsequent sections.
Biochemical signals regulating myofibroblast activation
Transforming growth factor-β1 (TGFβ1) is one of the first, best-
described and most potent cytokines known to induce myofibro-
blast activation83. Because extracellular activation of TGFβ1, which 
is kept inactive in complex with its latency-associated pro-peptide 
(LAP) (Fig. 2a), by fibroblastic cells has a mechanical component, it 
will be discussed below under mechanotransduction mechanisms 
for myofibroblast activation. In addition to TGFβ1, other TGFβ family 
members, including activin, TGFβ2 and TGFβ3, have all been shown 
to drive myofibroblast activation once liberated from their respective 
LAPs by assembling and binding to the same TGFβ receptor84. The trans-
membrane TGFβ receptor complex is formed by heterodimerization 
of the TGFβ receptor type I (Alk5; also known as TGFR1) and the TGFβ 
type II receptor (TGFR2)85–87. Its signalling can be enhanced by associ-
ating with co-receptors such as endoglin (CD105) or the TGFβ type III 
receptor (betaglycan; also known as TGFR3). Canonical TGFβ signalling 
is mediated through serine and/or threonine phosphorylation of the 
receptor-regulated SMAD proteins SMAD2 and SMAD3, which form a 
complex with SMAD4 after phosphorylation that can translocate to the 
nucleus (Fig. 2b). Binding of the SMAD2–SMAD3–SMAD4 complex to 
SMAD-binding elements in the promoter of pro-fibrotic genes such as 
the gene encoding αSMA enhances their transcription85–87.
The TGFβ1 pathway is neither strictly linear nor does it act in iso-
lation but rather contributes to a complex signalling network. The 
SMAD2–SMAD3–SMAD4 complex also acts as a co-transcription factor 
together with other factors such as Yes-associated protein 1 (YAP) and 
transcriptional co-activator with PDZ-binding motif (TAZ, together 
referred to as YAP/TAZ), myocardin-related transcription factor A 
(MRTFA; also known as MKL)88, as well as T cell factor (TCF) and Wnt 
signalling-related lymphoid enhancer factor (LEF)89. Jointly, they pro-
mote the transcription of myofibroblast-associated genes such as those 
encoding αSMA and collagen type I. In addition to canonical SMAD-
dependent signalling, tyrosine kinase activities of the TGFβ receptor 
complex also act non-canonically through MAPK–ERK, RAF–MEK1/2–
ERK1/2, p38 MAPK, Jun kinase, NF-κB, PI3K–AKT–mTOR, and Rho– 
Rho-associated kinase (ROCK)90,91 to drive the expression of ECM 
and/or contraction-related genes (Fig. 2b). The ROCK, YAP/TAZ and 
MRTFA pathways will be discussed below in the context of myofi-
broblast mechanosignalling. Beyond TGFβ1, numerous biochemical 
factors and their receptors have been studied for their function in 
controlling myofibroblast activation — often from the perspective of 
their potential use as therapeutic targets. Most prominently considered 
are IL-4, IL-6, IL-11, IL-13, angiotensin II, endothelin 1, platelet-derived 
growth factor (PDGF), lysophosphatidic acid receptor 1 and TLR4 
(refs. 92–95).
Mechanical myofibroblast activation
Many cell or tissue culture and animal experimental models (Supple-
mentary Boxes S1–S3) of tissue injury and fibrosis are based on the 
activation of myofibroblasts by mechanical stress96 (Supplementary 
Tables S1 and S2). Among various mechanical factors, the stress exerted 
by the ‘stiffness’ of wound and fibrotic ECM is a major driver of myofi-
broblast activation72,97,98. Atomic force microscopy is the most widely 
used technique to measure the elastic moduli (in pascal)99 of different 
normal human and rodent organs, including skin (0.1–10 kPa), brain 
(0. 1–0.5 kPa), fat tissue (1–3 kPa), lungs (0.5–3 kPa), liver (1–2 kPa), 
kidney, striated skeletal and heart muscles (10–15 kPa), and the stiff 
tissues building teeth and bone (1–4 GPa)100. An overview of the mod-
uli measured for these normal and fibrotic organs is provided in an 
excellent recent review100. The changes in ECM stiffness that occur 
after injury are key signals for fibroblasts to adopt their activity and 
activation state to the progress of repair, which is best studied in skin 
healing10,101. When the protective ECM architecture is lost after injury, 
external strains and stresses are directly transmitted to tissue-resident 
cells. Although not proven in vivo, it is tempting to speculate that the 
initial loss of structural integrity in damaged tissues is a mechanical 
trigger for migratory and ECM secretion programmes in fibroblastic 
cells residing at the wound edge. This idea is consistent with the concept 
of tensional homeostasis, that is, the observation that fibroblasts re-
establish their baseline tensional states by contracting after experimen-
tal relaxation or by relaxing following experimental stretching in 3D 
collagen ECM5,102. In addition to changes in mechanical stress, physical 
damage may generate ECM fragments and epitopes that serve as danger 
signals to activate fibroblasts and as biomarkers for clinicians103–106.
Before these pre-activated resident fibroblasts are activated and 
migrate into the wound bed, the wound gap is typically filled with 
a platelet-rich, fibrin-rich ECM10,107 (Fig. 1a). This fibrin clot provides a 
‘sticky’ reservoir for growth factors of platelets that release their cyto-
plasmic content after exposure to ECM, and rapidly stops bleeding if 
vessels have lost integrity. From a mechanical point of view, fibrin-rich 
ECM is initially approximately ten times softer than normal skin, with 
an elastic modulus of 0.1–1.0 kPa (ref. 108) (softer than cooked glass 
noodles, which curiously are much softer than overcooked spaghetti) 
but undergoes substantial strain stiffening through the contraction of 
activated platelets109. Thus, fibrin-rich ECM not only provides a scaf-
fold for fibroblasts to migrate into the wound bed but also affords the 
mechanical resistance required for the initiation of fibroblast contrac-
tile features (that is, formation of stress fibres). Whereas the provisional 
fibrin-rich ECM starts to be degraded shortly after its formation, fibro-
blasts begin to restore and maintain tissue integrity within a few days 
by producing an ECM rich in cellular fibronectin, as well as type III and 
type I collagen10. The low pulling forces of migratory fibroblasts 
and proto-myofibroblasts gradually stiffen the ECM to reach elastic 
moduli of ~20 kPa (similar to a marshmallow). Initial ECM stiffening is 
further promoted by inflammatory cell-mediated fibre crosslinking110; 

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
624
Review article
curiously, the release of ECM fibre tension (for example, after injury) 
seems to favour the recruitment of crosslinking enzymes such as tissue-
resident transglutaminases111. These processes accumulate over time 
and higher contractile forces are required to further remodel the now 
more mature scar; at this mechanical checkpoint, collagen-producing 
and low-contractile proto-myofibroblasts transition to the highly 
contractile αSMA-positive myofibroblasts44 (Fig. 1b).
Transient ECM stiffening is required to protect wound tissue from 
rupture, but the dysregulated healing of untreated large-area wounds 
and/or chronic myofibroblast activation inevitably result in contrac-
tures. In skin, the stiffness of fibrotic (hypertrophic) scar ECM resulting 
from continued myofibroblast activities can reach elastic moduli of 
50–100 kPa, that is, a hundred times stiffer than normal skin112 (almost 
as stiff as a middle-aged gouda cheese). Increases in stiffness by one to 
two orders of magnitude are also reported for the fibrotic ECM accu-
mulating in other organs100. ECM stiffness is further enhanced through 
chemical crosslinks established by fibroblastic and inflammatory 
cell-derived transglutaminases113, lysyl hydroxylases114, lysyl oxidases115 
and lysyl oxidase-like enzymes116. Consequently, fibrotic ECM is not 
only highly dense but also more resistant to resolution by proteolytic 
enzymes117,118. In addition to obstructing the function of the affected 
organ (for example, lung expansion, heart pumping, kidney blood 
filtration), stiff fibrotic scar tissue further drives the progression of 
fibrosis by turning healthy precursor cells into fibrotic myofibroblasts, 
thereby establishing a self-perpetuating fibrotic feedback loop101,119. 
It is one of the most daunting tasks and currently unreached goals of
