Review article
anti-fibrotic therapies to break this loop, which has evolved multiple 
redundancies to promote rapid healing of injured tissues at the expense 
of functionality72. Attractive targets for anti-fibrotic strategies are 
found in the molecular mechanisms that closely tie myofibroblast acti-
vation to their mechanical environment. This includes cell components 
that sense ECM mechanics and transduce the generated signals into 
myofibroblast-promoting transcription programmes. Other potential 
therapeutic targets are presented by the ECM in a stress-dependent 
manner, including cryptic recognition sites that open after strain for 
the binding of cell receptors and other ECM ligands but remain hidden 
under homeostatic conditions. Another ECM stiffness-dependent and 
strain-dependent event in the myofibroblast activation cascade is the 
release of active TGFβ1 from ECM latent stores. Mechanical activation 
of TGFβ1 and revelation of cryptic ECM domains will be discussed in 
the next sections.
Mechanisms of (myo-)fibroblast 
mechanoperception
Fibroblastic cells can sense and respond to mechanical cues emanating 
from the extracellular milieu through different cell surface receptors 
such as integrins, discoidin domain receptors (DDR), G protein-coupled 
receptors (GPCR) and stretch-activated ion channels (SACs)120. All these 
receptor systems activate mechanotransduction signalling pathways120 
that ultimately regulate fibroblast activation states either acutely, for 
instance by promoting the polymerization of actin into filaments and 
stress fibres, or at the transcription level.
ECM receptors of fibroblasts and myofibroblasts
Fibroblastic cells transmit forces to contract ECM into scar tissue and 
probe the chemical and mechanical properties of their environment 
by physically linking their contractile cytoskeleton to transmembrane 
ECM adhesion receptor complexes121. The most prominent fibro-
blast ECM receptors include the hyaluronic acid receptor CD44, DDR1 
and DDR2, and various integrins. Hyaluronic acid binding to CD44 
induces the formation of adhesion complexes of adaptor proteins that 
link to the contractile actin cytoskeleton122. Activation of CD44 and its 
downstream effectors are critical to fibroblast motility and invasion 
in response to stiffening ECM and blockage of CD44 abrogates stiff 
remodelling in the fibrotic lung and post-infarct heart123.
DDR1 has increasingly attracted the attention of researchers 
due to its specific binding and compaction of fibrillar collagen in a 
mechanically regulated and myosin-mediated process124. Expression 
of DDR1 on fibroblastic cells is enhanced in tissues affected by fibro-
proliferative disorders, including cancer, skin hypertrophic scars, liver, 
kidney and lung fibrosis, and it is thus considered a drug target125,126. 
Analogously, integrin β1 can bind to different α-integrins to form α1β1, 
α2β1, α10β1 and α11β1 heterodimers, all of which have been described 
as fibroblast collagen receptors involved in tissue repair, for instance, 
of skin and heart98,127. Because of their enriched, if not exclusive, expres-
sion on mesenchymal myofibroblast precursor cells, integrins α11β1 
and α1β8 are being scrutinized in experimental animal models for their 
potential to inhibit fibrosis in the liver, lungs and cancer stroma128–130. 
Functional deletion of integrin α11β1, but not of the other collagen 
receptor integrins α1β1 and α2β1, resulted in reduced fibroblast acti-
vation and reduced skin scarring131. Despite showing some specificity 
for fibroblastic cells, integrin α1β8 can bind multiple ECM ligands and 
its pro-fibrotic mode of action is just beginning to be unravelled130.
Part of the binding promiscuity of integrin α1β8 is due to its recog-
nition of the arginine–glycine–aspartate (RGD) motif, which is present 
in multiple ECM proteins such as in vitronectin and fibronectin, which 
Fig. 2 | Signalling pathways regulating myofibroblast activation. 
a, Fibroblasts secrete transforming growth factor-β1 (TGFβ1) in complex 
with its latency-associated peptide (LAP); this latent complex can be stored 
in the extracellular matrix (ECM) by covalent binding to latent TGFβ1-binding 
protein 1 (LTBP1). Within the mechanically resistant ECM characteristic of 
tissues under repair, cell contractile forces transmitted through intracellular 
actomyosin bundles (stress fibres) and αv integrins to arginine–glycine–
aspartate (RGD)-binding domains in LAP induce a conformational change in the 
latent TGFβ1 ‘straitjacket’ conformation that releases active TGFβ1. b, Active 
TGFβ1 binds to and assembles the TGFβ receptor complex and promotes 
canonical SMAD signalling, resulting in the translocation of SMAD4–SMAD2–
SMAD3 complexes to the nucleus, where they regulate the transcription of genes 
such as ACTA2 (encoding α-smooth muscle actin). This action is counteracted 
by the negative regulator SMAD7. In addition, TGFβ1 binding initiates non-
canonical signalling through JNK, p38, ERK, MAPK and Rho–Rho-associated 
kinase (ROCK) signalling. c, Extracellular binding of integrins in the presence of 
mechanical stress (for example, stiff ECM) results in an integrin conformational 
change, which starts a recruitment cascade of structural and signalling proteins 
to the cytoplasmic portion of integrins, resulting in the formation of adhesion 
complexes. Focal adhesion proteins involved in fibroblast mechanotransduction 
include the focal adhesion kinase (FAK; not shown), vinculin, kindlin and talin, 
which mediate binding to actin filaments (F-actin) and facilitate the formation 
of stress fibres. d, Biochemical and biomechanical myofibroblast signalling 
pathways intersect at multiple junctures and create a complex signalling 
network in which the state of actin polymerization takes centre stage. In addition 
to being a downstream target of TGFβ1 receptor signalling, ROCK is activated 
by the GTP-bound form of RhoA following the binding of lysophosphatidic acid 
(LPA) and thrombin to G protein-coupled receptors (GPCR). A key downstream 
event of ROCK activation is enhanced fibroblast contraction via inhibition of 
myosin light chain phosphatase (MLCP) and activation of myosin light chain 
kinase (MLCK). Rho GTPases also directly affect actin polymerization by 
regulating actin monomer-binding and polymer-binding proteins (not shown 
here). The status of actin polymerization further controls gene transcription 
through regulation of mechanosensitive co-transcription factors such as 
myocardin-related transcription factor A (MRTFA). Actin polymerization results 
in the release of MRTFA from globular (G)-actin and allows MRTFA translocation 
into the nucleus. Association of nuclear MRTFA with serum response factor (SRF) 
drives the transcription of pro-fibrotic gene products such as CCN2 and ACTA2. 
e, In addition, actin polymerization and contraction control gene expression 
by regulating the nuclear localization of the transcriptional co-activator with 
PDZ-binding motif (TAZ) and Yes-associated protein (YAP). For instance, RhoA 
activation and subsequent formation of stress fibres leads to the inhibition 
of large tumour suppressor homologue 1 (LATS1) and LATS2 in the cytosol. 
LATS1 and LATS2 are part of the Hippo signalling pathway and downstream 
targets of the mammalian STE20-like kinase 1 (MST1), MST2 and the scaffolding 
protein SAV. Inhibition of LATS1 and LATS2 increases the fraction of cytosolic 
unphosphorylated YAP and TAZ. Phosphorylated YAP and TAZ are prone to 
proteasomal degradation in complex with protein 14-3-3 and the ubiquitin 
ligase βTrCP. In contrast, non-phosphorylated YAP and TAZ translocate into the 
nucleus where they associate with co-transcription factors such as TEA domain 
family of DNA-binding proteins (TEAD), β-catenin and the T cell factor–lymphoid 
enhancing factor (TCF–LEF) to drive the transcription of pro-fibrotic genes such 
as CCN2 and microRNA-21. Note that transcription factor-binding sites are not 
drawn in the correct sequence and not all transcription factors are required at 
the same time to drive pro-fibrotic gene expression. PAR1, proteinase-activated 
receptor 1.

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
626
Review article
contribute to the formation of provisional ECM after injury, and in peri-
ostin, thrombospondins and fibrillins, which are enriched in fibrotic 
ECM132,133. Accordingly, other RGD-recognizing integrins, such as α5β1, 
αvβ3 and αvβ5, were also shown to mediate fibroblast mechanosensing, 
force transmission and myofibroblast activation134–136. However, their 
potential as specific myofibroblast targets is limited by their expression 
on multiple other, unrelated cell types. The RGD binding motif is also 
a key recognition site found in the latent TGFβ1–LAP complex. Here, 
the RGD motif mediates high-affinity binding to integrins αvβ1 (mainly 
found on fibroblastic cells)137, αvβ6 (only epithelial cells)138 and αvβ8 
(mainly immune cells)139, all of which were shown to activate latent 
TGFβ1 in the extracellular space in vitro and in animal models of fibro-
sis85,86 (Fig. 2a). How fibroblastic cells use contractile force to activate 
latent TGFβ1 held in complex with LAP in the ECM will be covered in the 
next section after discussing the role of mechanical stress in enhancing 
fibroblast adhesion as a prerequisite for myofibroblast activation.
Fibroblasts contract stronger on stiffer substrates in an attempt 
to maintain tensional homeostasis, that is, equilibrium between ECM 
resistance and intracellular force140. Consequently, the strength of 
their adhesions must be adapted to withstand higher stresses. Limiting 
adhesion maturation by restricting the substrate surfaces available for 
fibroblast attachment, substrate adhesiveness or stiffness are used 
as experimental strategies to reduce intracellular stress and myofi-
broblast activation135. For example, patterning of body implants with 
adhesive micro-islands has been explored as an approach to prevent 
myofibroblast activation and fibrosis at the surface of body implants141. 
Mechanical stress directly regulates the binding affinity of fibroblastic 
integrins by a strain-induced conformational change in the β-integrin 
subunit, which initiates a cascade of intracellular signalling events 
that culminate in the maturation of adhesion sites in terms of size and 
strength from small focal complexes into mature focal adhesions and 
supermature adhesions in myofibroblasts121 (Fig. 1b). Supermature focal 
adhesions have been described as ECM adhesions 10–20 µm in length 
specific to αSMA-positive cultured myofibroblasts142. Compared to the 
2–6-µm long focal adhesions of αSMA-negative proto-myofibroblasts, 
they can sustain higher stress, that is, force per area, and thus allow 
the formation of highly contractile intracellular stress fibres135. Struc-
tures equivalent to supermature adhesions, called fibronexus, have 
been described in different tissue-residing myofibroblasts, first in the 
granulation tissue of healing guinea pig skin wounds143.
Among the hundreds of molecular components that are recruited 
during the maturation of integrin adhesions, talin and kindlin fam-
ily members take centre stage by directly binding to integrin tails 
and the actin cytoskeleton in a stress-dependent manner144,145 (Fig. 2). 
Kindlin 2 expression is elevated in experimental models of kidney, 
lung, heart and skin fibrosis, and functional loss of kindlin 2 prevents 
mechanical activation of fibroblasts cultured from these tissues146,147. 
Focal adhesion kinase (FAK) and integrin-linked kinase (ILK; which, 
ironically, has no kinase activity and does not bind to integrins) are 
other mechanosensitive key components of fibroblast adhesions. 
Experimentally induced loss of ILK reduces fibroblast force transduc-
tion, TGFβ1 secretion, myofibroblast activation and myofibroblast-
mediated tissue contraction in mouse skin and kidney fibrosis, possibly 
by loss of focal adhesion structure148,149. Likewise, loss of FAK func-
tion in cultured fibroblasts reduces myofibroblast activation150 and 
thereby alleviates fibrotic features in mouse models of hypertrophic 
scarring151. Given that these signalling and structural components are 
expressed in all adhesive cells, targeting their function with general 
inhibitors rather than fibroblast-specific genetic knockout bears the 
risk of side effects. Higher specificity can be expected for the targeting 
of fibroblast-specific integrins that are differentially expressed in tissue 
repair conditions, such as integrin α11β1, and has been shown for the 
αv integrins involved in fibroblast-mediated activation of latent TGFβ1 
(refs. 152,153), which we discuss next.
Mechanochemical crosstalk in myofibroblast activation
Although typically thought of as a soluble cue that exhibits its effects 
through cell–cell paracrine signalling, activation of TGFβ is intimately 
tied to the mechanical state of the ECM and fibroblasts. Fibroblas-
tic cells secrete latent TGFβ1 in complex with its pro-peptide LAP. In 
contrast to the pro-peptides of other secreted factors that are typi-
cally cleaved off during secretion, LAP is cleaved intracellularly by 
furin enzymes but remains non-covalently associated with the TGFβ1 
cytokine after secretion. This association traps the TGFβ1 cytokine 
in an inactive ‘straitjacket’ configuration154. LAP covalently binds to 
the fibrillin protein family member latent TGFβ1-binding protein 1 
(LTBP1), thereby enabling long-term storage of latent TGFβ1 in the 
ECM155. Other cells, notably T regulatory cells of the adaptive immune 
system, use the same covalent binding domain in LAP to store latent 
TGFβ1 in the extracellular space but, instead of using LTBP1, they link 
LAP to the membrane proteins TGFβ activators LRRC32 or LRRC33 
(refs. 156,157). Several publications have shown and reviewed how 
different cell types use different mechanisms of latent TGFβ1 presen-
tation and activation, thereby achieving precise control over the local 
TGFβ1 availability85,86,158. For ECM-bound latent TGFβ1, the integrins 
αvβ1, αvβ3, αvβ5 and αvβ6 have all been demonstrated to mechanically 
activate latent TGFβ1 by transmitting pulling forces to the RGD domain 
in LAP152,153 (Fig. 2a,b). Consequently, specific inhibition or genetic 
knockout of the latent TGFβ1-binding fibroblast integrins successfully 
inhibits the development of fibrosis in different experimental animal 
models137,138,153,159,160. Unlike the LAP of TGFβ1, the latent pro-peptide 
of TGFβ2, another pro-fibrotic actor and myofibroblast-activating 
cytokine, lacks the αv integrin–RGD binding site161,162. It remains unclear 
whether integrins have a role in TGFβ2 activation, but recent studies 
support the hypothesis that αvβ6 integrins on activated lung epithelial 
cells bind to a specialized binding motif in pro-TGFβ2 and contribute to 
TGFβ2 activation in a strain-dependent process161,162. Cell-pulling forces 
exercised on the latent TGFβ1–LTBP1 complex must meet a mechani-
cally resisting, that is, sufficiently pre-strained and remodelled, ECM 
to be able to induce a conformational change in the LAP–TGFβ1 strait-
jacket configuration and to liberate the active TGFβ1 cytokine163. Nota-
bly, the (in vitro) stiffness enabling TGFβ1 activation by fibroblasts is 
lower (~3 kPa)152 than that required for αSMA expression in stress fibres 
(~16 kPa)135. Therefore, mechanical activation of TGFβ1 serves as a first 
mechanical checkpoint for full myofibroblast activation.
Structural changes in ECM proteins under strain often reveal 
new protein motifs that function as signals for fibroblastic cells in 
the mechanical conditions of tissue under repair but are hidden when 
no strain is applied. For example, fibronectin has a domain structure 
perfectly adapted to orchestrate complex repair scenarios. It serves 
as a hub for multiple cytokines and growth factors and as an initial 
structural template to guide the assembly of other ECM proteins, 
including collagens, fibrillins and LTBP1 (ref. 164). Differential splic-
ing of cellular fibronectin produces the extradomain (type III) vari-
ants ED-B and ED-A fibronectin164,165. The latter is produced only in 
fibroproliferative conditions, is a prerequisite molecule for myofibro-
blast activation166, and presents binding sites for the integrins α4β7, 
α4β1 and α9β1 (ref. 133). Other ‘cryptic’ binding epitopes outside the 

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
627
Review article
extradomain variants may become accessible only in the presence of 
ED-A and/or ED-B, as proposed for LTBP1 binding167. Notably, cryp-
tic fibronectin self-assembly and signalling sites are revealed after 
mechanical strain168,169 — a feature that can promote myofibroblast 
activation even in a soft environment134. Primary lung fibroblasts cul-
tured on very soft (~680 Pa) fibronectin-rich ECM display increased 
spreading, contractility and nuclear translocation of the canonical 
myofibroblast transcription factor myocardin-related transcription 
factor A (MRTFA)134. This myofibroblast-promoting mechanoresponse 
is in stark contrast to the expectation of myofibroblast suppression on 
soft substrates and can be explained by a strain-induced conforma-
tional change in the central cell integrin receptor-binding region of 
fibronectin. Applying strain to fibronectin is proposed to prompt an 
‘integrin mechano-switch’ from α5β1 integrin to αvβ3 integrin bind-
ing in this domain134. Elevated binding of integrin αvβ3 then allows 
myofibroblast activation in what would otherwise be considered a 
mechanically impermissible environment134. Biophysical analysis of 
fibronectin has demonstrated that, after relaxation of mechanical 
forces, such as those generated by activated fibroblasts, it is capable 
of fully recovering its original conformation170,171, thus flipping the 
switch back towards α5β1 integrin binding. It is tempting to specu-
late that force-mediated conformational changes in fibronectin, like 
the integrin mechano-switch, are leveraged during normal tissue repair 
to allow fast adaptations of fibroblast activities to acute and local 
changes in the mechanical environment.
Myofibroblast stretch-activated channels
Enhanced mechanical strain is also the basis for the activation of SACs 
localized to the fibroblast membrane to coordinate myofibroblast 
activation and activity in response to extracellular stress172,173. The 
SAC transient receptor potential cation channel subfamily V mem-
ber 4 (TRPV4) co-localizes with both integrin β1 and DDR1 adhesions, 
where it regulates fibroblast ECM remodelling activities by controlling 
the local influx of Ca2+ ions174,175. TRPV4 is also important for stress-
mediated and TGFβ1-mediated myofibroblast activation of skin, lung 
and cardiac fibroblasts, and loss of TRPV4 function protects mouse 
hearts from experimentally induced fibrosis176–178. Similarly, myofi-
broblast activation of dermal fibroblasts is regulated by TRPV3 in a 
process dependent on SMAD2 and SMAD3 in vitro and in vivo179. One 
decade after the discovery of Piezo1 and Piezo2 as key components 
of another class of SACs180, a role for Piezo1 in regulating fibroblast 
repair functions is beginning to emerge. Mechanical stimulation of 
Piezo1 elicits downstream signalling that culminates in myofibroblast 
activation in skin hypertrophic scarring181, kidney fibrosis182 and heart 
fibrosis183. Like TRPV4, Piezo1 has been found in adhesions, where it 
contributes to fibroblast sensing of ECM stiffness184. SACs of yet unre-
solved molecular composition are further involved in coordinating 
the contractile activities of small groups of myofibroblasts that are 
physically connected by actin-associated adherens junctions185. In 
cell–cell and cell–ECM adhesions, SACs are ideally positioned to adapt 
fibroblast-remodelling activities in close feedback with the mechani-
cal state of the ECM and their neighbouring cells by coordinating Ca2+ 
influx, downstream Rho-associated protein kinase (ROCK) signalling 
and actin–myosin activity186.
Mechanisms of myofibroblast 
mechanotransduction
So far, we have discussed how fibroblastic cells perceive mechanical 
signals and how initial biochemical signals, such as active TGFβ1 or 
high cytosolic Ca2+, arise from mechanical events. Before establishing 
the link between myofibroblast mechanosensing and their activation 
at the level of transcription, we will provide a brief overview of how 
the same signals acutely regulate myofibroblast contractile activity 
in response to changes in the mechanical environment. More details 
on the regulation of fibroblastic cell contraction can be obtained from 
our recent review specifically on the topic72.
Calcium, Rho, ROCK and myofibroblast contraction
Myofibroblast contraction is principally controlled by myosin light 
chain (MLC) kinase (MLCK)-mediated phosphorylation of the myosin 
light chains (MLCs) associated with non-muscle myosin 2 (MYH2)187,188. 
Phosphorylated myosin heads bind to actin filaments within stress 
fibres and produce force in the form of a power stroke, not unlike 
the contraction of the much higher organized sarcomeres of mus-
cle cells72,189 (Fig. 2). Conversely, de-phosphorylation of MLC by the 
MLC phosphatase (MLCP) results in myosin unbinding from actin fila-
ments, which terminates the contraction cycle72,189. Myofibroblasts 
exhibit at least two modes of contraction that are regulated by either 
enhancing MLCK activity through Ca2+ signalling or inhibiting MLCP 
activity through RhoA–ROCK signalling187,188 (Fig. 2d). We previously 
proposed a lock-step model in which both contraction modes act in 
concert to locally release ECM fibres from stress to allow their shorten-
ing while maintaining overall ECM integrity5. According to this model, 
RhoA–ROCK signalling controls longer-lasting (hours), long-ranging 
(micrometre) and strong (micro-Newtons) contractile tone that creates 
and maintains slack in ECM fibres against a tense environment. Stress-
released ECM fibres are then amenable to Ca2+-mediated contractions 
that are smaller in range (nanometre) and force (nano-Newtons) but 
occur regularly and last a few minutes each187. Contractile amplitude 
and frequency are acutely adapted to increasing extracellular resistance 
by enhancing the frequency of Ca2+ oscillations and RhoA activities190,191. 
The transiently contracted ECM state is proposed to be established by 
proteolytic processing and crosslinking of collagen fibres–at this point, 
the load can now be transferred from the contracted myofibroblast 
to the shortened ECM, and myofibroblasts can re-spread for a new 
cycle. This hypothesis is supported by experimental observations that 
collagen fibres are protected from proteolytic cleavage in collagenase-
rich environments when under an experimental mechanical load but 
dissolve when relaxed117.
Of the three Rho isoforms, RhoA is best studied in the context of 
myofibroblast activation, whereas the roles of RhoB and RhoC are yet to 
be resolved192. Likewise, ROCK1 and ROCK2 both control myofibroblast 
contractile force and activation in mechanically stressed environ-
ments and are considered potential therapeutic targets in lung and 
heart fibrosis193–195. However, given that Ca2+, Rho and ROCK control 
many cellular processes and regulate the physiological contraction of 
other cell types in fibrotic organs (for example, cardiomyocytes in the 
heart, airway SMCs in lungs and vascular SMCs in all organs), specific 
intervention with myofibroblast contraction should rather be imple-
mented upstream of these central signalling pathways. For instance, 
ligand binding to the G protein-coupled receptors (GPCR) lysophos-
phatidic acid (LPA) receptor 1 or proteinase-activated receptor 1 (PAR1) 
has been described to contribute to myofibroblast activation and 
enhanced contraction through RhoA–ROCK196–198. The associated 
G proteins that initiate pro-fibrotic signalling cascades are Gαi/o, Gαq11, 
Gα12/13 and Gαs (refs. 162,199). Conversely, the oestrogen GPCR and 
the G protein Gαs were shown to have anti-fibrotic functions and can 
be stimulated to suppress myofibroblast activation in experimental 

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
628
Review article
models of fibrosis200. Notably, these signalling functions of GPCRs 
can relate to ROCK-mediated contraction but also intersect with other 
pathways, such as JAK2–STAT3 (ref. 201), nuclear factor of activated 
T cells (NFAT)202 and YAP/TAZ signalling199, to regulate myofibroblast 
activation at the level of transcription.
YAP, TAZ and YAP/TAZ
YAP and TAZ were originally discovered as members of the Hippo path-
way that can become activated by various upstream stimuli, includ-
ing those relevant for myofibroblast activation and fibrosis such as 
Wnt and GPCR ligands203. The Hippo signalling pathway consists of 
a cascade of mammalian STE20-like kinase 1 (MST1; also known as 
serine–threonine-protein kinase 4), MST2, large tumour suppressor 
homologue 1 (LATS1) and LATS2 (Fig. 2e). The phosphorylated and thus 
activated serine–threonine kinases LATS1 and LATS2 phosphorylate 
YAP and TAZ, resulting in their cytoplasmic sequestration and/or pro-
teasomal degradation203. In addition to their role in Hippo signalling, 
YAP and TAZ have become widely known for their role as mechanosensi-
tive transcription factors. Both localize to the nucleus of fibroblasts 
grown on fibrosis-stiff culture substrates and under mechanical stress 
but remain in the cytosol in fibroblasts grown on physiologically soft 
culture substrates204 (Supplementary Box S1 and Supplementary 
Table S1). Activation of RhoA and subsequent formation of stress 
fibres leads to the inhibition of LATS1 and LATS2 in the cytoplasm and 
enhances the pool of cytosolic unphosphorylated YAP and TAZ205. 
Unphosphorylated YAP and TAZ can translocate into the nucleus and 
regulate target gene expression through interactions with members of 
the TEA domain family of DNA-binding proteins (TEAD), as well as with 
SMADs, p73 and Runt-related transcription factor 1 (RUNX), to mediate 
transcription of fibrosis-related genes. Among the YAP and TAZ target 
genes are CCN2 (mentioned above) and microRNA-21, which will be 
discussed further below in the context of myofibroblast memory206.
Sustained nuclear activity of YAP and TAZ drives transcription 
of ECM-associated and contraction-associated genes in myofibro-
blasts of the kidney, lung, liver and palmar fascia207. Early activation of 
YAP/TAZ during tissue repair may serve to prime fibroblasts to respond 
to TGFβ signals as nuclear YAP/TAZ promotes SMAD2 and SMAD3 
nuclear retainment208. The same studies demonstrated that inhibition 
of YAP/TAZ signalling reduces experimentally induced organ fibrosis. 
Although often considered as a pair, YAP and TAZ have both redundant 
and independent roles and, whereas single knockout of either factor 
reduces fibroblast activation, dual knockout induces a significantly 
more potent effect209,210. Although the full mechanism remains to be 
elucidated, the redundancy observed from single knockouts of YAP or 
TAZ may in part be mediated by compensatory activities of other TEAD-
binding co-factors in the nucleus. In addition to the above-described 
regulation of YAP and TAZ nuclear translocation, pro-fibrotic signals 
such as TGFβ1 (via SMAD3) can also regulate the expression levels — at 
least of TAZ — during myofibroblast activation. Curiously, regula-
tion of TAZ transcription is dependent on another mechanosensitive 
co-transcription factor, MRTFA211.
Myocardin-related transcription factor A
Analogous to YAP and TAZ, MRTFA translocates from the cytosol, 
where it is sequestered by binding to non-polymerized globular actin 
monomers, into the nucleus of mechanically stressed cells (Fig. 2). Once 
released from sequestration after stress-induced actin polymerization 
in the cytosol, MRTFA acts as a co-transcription factor in combination 
with serum response factor (SRF) to increase target gene transcrip-
tion211. MRTF–SRF target genes include the myofibroblast-associated 
genes CCN2 and ACTA2 (encoding αSMA) and genes encoding stress-
sensing and stress-transmitting cytoskeletal components, such as the 
integrins α2 and α11 (collagen binding), integrin αv (fibronectin and 
latent TGFβ1 binding) and the corresponding β-integrins β1, β3 and 
β5, as well as integrin-linked protein kinase (ILK) and TAZ148. MRTFA 
was first discovered as a driver of myofibroblast activation in cultured 
kidney tubular epithelial cells, where it contributes to epithelial–
mesenchymal transition and expression of αSMA in response to TGFβ1 
and injury212. Later, the same function was confirmed in various fibro-
blastic cells in conditions mimicking physiological and pathological 
repair213. Consequently, treatment of dermal wounds with the MRTFA 
agonist isoxazole increases the accumulation of αSMA-positive cells 
in granulation tissue and thereby accelerates normal wound closure214. 
Conversely, second-generation pharmacological inhibitors of MRTF–
SRF signalling, such as the compounds CCG-222740, CCG-100602 and 
CCG-203971, protect against cancer-associated pancreatic fibrosis215 
and intestinal216 and eye217 fibrosis, and mice with fibroblasts deficient 
in MRTFA and MRTFB are protected from renal fibrosis148. Exciting 
recent studies have started to establish a link between acute induction 
of myofibroblast transcription programmes by MRTFA and YAP/TAZ 
Glossary
Arginine–glycine–aspartate 
(RGD) motif
The RGD amino acid sequence is a 
motif in the latent transforming growth 
factor-β1 complex and many proteins 
of the extracellular matrix, where 
it promotes specific binding of αv 
integrins; the presence of an adjacent 
‘synergy sequence’ in fibronectin also 
allows integrins αIIbβ3 and α5β1 to bind 
to RGD.
Granulation tissue
This provisional tissue fills wounds 
during healing, is rich in various 
cells and small vessels, and appears 
in histological cross-sections as 
‘granulose’.
Pericytes
Also referred to as perivascular 
cells, pericytes show fibroblast-like 
morphology, are closely associated 
with endothelial cells of the 
microvasculature and often have 
progenitor capacity, that is, the ability 
to differentiate into multiple cell types, 
including fibroblasts.
Stiffness
Most biological studies dealing with 
cell, extracellular matrix or tissue 
‘stiffness’ refer to the elastic modulus 
(that is, stress over strain) in pascal, 
which is a relatively accurate parameter 
when measured at the length scale of 
cells and below.
Strain stiffening
In contrast to ideally elastic synthetic 
polymers, biological polymers such as 
collagen, fibrin and cytoskeletal filaments 
get stiffer when they are extended (that 
is, strained). Strain stiffening protects 
tissues from overstraining and enables 
the fibrous collagen around large blood 
vessels to undergo small peristaltic 
movements but to cuff the vessel under 
high blood pressure.
Stress fibres
Contractile bundles composed of actin 
and myosin filaments as part of the 
cytoskeleton that are typically formed 
in response to mechanical stress and 
often terminate in cell adhesions, which 
connect to the extracellular matrix or 
other cells.
Tropomyosin
Tropomyosins are filamentous (F-)
actin-binding proteins that regulate 
actin–myosin interactions in adult 
striated and smooth muscle, as well as 
in non-muscle contractile cells such  
as myofibroblasts.

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
629
Review article
and lasting myofibroblast activation by epigenetic reprogramming as 
discussed in the next section218.
Myofibroblast fates and memory
One of the central questions in fibrosis research is whether myofi-
broblasts in fibrotic lesions are fundamentally different to those in 
normally healing tissues. The authors of this Review argue that they 
are principally the same, that is, activated by the same molecular and 
mechanical mechanisms from the same precursor cells. However, 
myofibroblasts disappear at the end of normal tissue repair, that is, 
they are transient, but persist in fibrosis219 (Fig. 1a). In the following 
section, we will discuss how prolonged and continued exposure to 
a fibrotic environment generates epigenetic memory that converts 
transient myofibroblasts into persistent myofibroblasts. Initially, 
myofibroblasts were considered to represent a terminally ‘differenti-
ated’ fibroblast state and apoptosis was thought to be their ultimate 
fate219. However, it is now evident that myofibroblast deactivation 
and switching between different fibroblast activation states is 
possible, including reversal of the highly activated αSMA-positive 
myofibroblast phenotype to proto-myofibroblasts or stress fibre-
negative fibroblastic cells220. Deactivation seems to be the prominent 
myofibroblast clearance mechanism during liver repair35, whereas 
myofibroblast apoptosis prevails in repaired skin wounds221, as in 
most organs. Both apoptosis and necroptosis have been described 
to reduce the numbers of cardiac myofibroblasts and both processes 
have been experimentally targeted in vivo to improve heart repair 
after injury222,223. We and others have reviewed how myofibroblast 
survival is regulated by their acquired resistance to death and how 
this resistance is controlled by mechanical factors219,224. Here, we will 
focus on the regulation of myofibroblast fate through epigenetic 
modifications, that is, heritable but reversible phenotypic alterations 
mediated by non-coding RNAs or more stable modifications at the 
level of the DNA and histones. Epigenetic imprinting and maintenance 
of myofibroblast features have long been recognized as a reason for 
myofibroblast persistence in fibrosis225,226 but myofibroblast epige-
netic control through mechanical factors is a comparably young and 
unexplored field of research.
Mechanical memory promotes myofibroblast persistence
Earlier work has discovered that prolonged exposure, or ‘priming’, of 
lung fibroblasts and MSCs to pathophysiologically stiff and soft culture 
substrates imprints myofibroblast activation states that persist even 
after the switch to a different mechanical environment227,228. We accord-
ingly coined the term ‘mechanical memory’. The duration of mechanical 
priming (hours–weeks) and the strength of the mechanical stimulus 
(dosing), for example, the magnitude of the substrate elastic modulus, 
affect the duration of memory. Notably, there is no consensus on the 
values of elastic moduli to label substrates as ‘soft’ or ‘stiff’ — both are 
used relative to each other and to the normal physiological environ-
ment of the cells; here, we align with the definitions given in the respec-
tive studies. For guidance on the elastic moduli measured for normal 
(approximately 0.1–5 kPa) and fibrotic connective tissues (>10 kPa), 
see our section on mechanical myofibroblast activation above.
Mechanical memory was later reported to also stabilize bone 
and fat lineage choices of MSCs on stiff and soft substrates229 and 
MSCs subjected to dynamic tensile loading on nanofibrous scaf-
folds230. MSCs are frequently chosen as a model to study mechanical 
regulation of cell fate, which includes MSC–myofibroblast activation, 
either as a pre-state of osteogenesis or as a fibrogenic lineage choice231. 
Concomitantly, mechanical memory of prior experience with a stiff 
culture environment was shown to maintain the myofibroblast activa-
tion state of other cells of MSC lineage such as chondrocytes232, which 
reduces their suitability for subsequent grafting to heal destroyed 
cartilage. Mechanical reprogramming was proposed to ‘rejuvenate’ 
mesenchymal cells, that is, restoring their potential to repair dam-
aged tissues instead of producing adverse scar tissue233. It seems that 
mechanical stress hierarchically engages acute mechanotransduction 
first and later promotes epigenetic modifications to generate memory 
depending on the duration (dose) and intensity (amplitude) of the 
stimulus234. For instance, mechanical priming of human MSCs for 
3–10 days on stiff surfaces (10 kPa) generates transient memory main-
tained by YAP-mediated gene expression programmes229. In contrast, 
‘stiff-priming’ (100 kPa) for 3 weeks establishes high levels of micro-
RNA-21, which drive pro-fibrotic programmes for 2 weeks following the 
removal of mechanical stress228. The duration of mechanical memory 
in these studies likely depended on the retention time of YAP in the 
nucleus (hours) and the turnover rates of microRNA-21 (days).
Exposure to a stiff mechanical environment can imprint even 
longer-lasting, yet reversible, epigenetic modifications made at the 
level of histones and DNA. Repeated tensile loading of bovine MSCs 
resulted in enlarged cell nuclei and decreased chromatin conden-
sation235. How long these features are maintained after mechanical 
unloading depends on the duration, number and amplitude of the 
priming loading cycles and on the histone methyltransferase (MET) 
EZH2 (ref. 235). Human and mouse lung fibroblasts cultured on stiff 
polymer (64-kPa) and stiff plastic (gigapascal) substrates show higher 
methylation levels of histone H3 at lysine residue K9 (H3K9me) than 
those of fibroblasts cultured on soft (0.4-kPa) substrates236. Human 
MSCs grown for 3 days on stiff substrates (33 kPa) have larger nuclei, 
lower chromatin condensation, higher expression of histone dea-
cetylases (HDACs) and lower expression of histone acetyltransferases 
(HATs) than that of soft-grown MSCs (5.5 kPa)237; later, these changes 
were shown to be associated with higher histone acetylation and MSC 
osteogenesis238. Expression levels of the epigenetic modifiers HDAC 
and HAT adapt to new mechanical environments after only 1 day of 
priming but stabilize after 10 days of priming237. The same authors 
reported myofibroblast memory acquired by porcine heart valve fibro-
blasts, primed on a moderately stiff (4-kPa) substrate for 2–7 days and 
then switched to a softer substrate (2 kPa) for 2 days239,240. In these 
experiments, maintenance of myofibroblast features after stiff–soft 
switch also depended on the priming duration. Stiff-grown fibroblasts 
exhibited specific open chromatin regions that contained binding 
motifs for transcription factors driving mechanosensing pathways and 
myofibroblast gene expression, including αSMA239. Global chromatin 
condensation was found to acutely (1 day) decrease after exposure 
to stiff substrates but then gradually increase over time (3–9 days); 
this observation was explained with nuclear stiffening in response to 
higher stress on resistant substrates239. Indeed, there is a linear scaling 
relation between collagen content (and thus, ECM stiffness) and lamin 
A contents in the nuclear envelope across different stages of fibrosis, 
indicating that ECM conditions directly reflect nuclear mechanics and 
pro-fibrotic signalling241.
Changes in the chromatin state of cells cultured in different 
mechanical environments are mediated by a direct link between sub-
strate and nuclear mechanics242 (Fig. 3a,b). Critical for extracellular–
intranuclear force transmission from substrate adhesions are 
intact actin and intermediate filaments — vimentin in the case of 
fibroblasts — and their interaction with the linker of nucleoskeleton

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
631
Review article
and cytoskeleton (LINC) complex in the outer nuclear membrane. For 
recent reviews on nuclear structures and nuclear mechanotransduc-
tion, see refs. 243–245. The LINC complex spans the nuclear envelope 
and is composed of nesprins and Sad1 and UNC-84 (SUN) proteins. 
The LINC complex binds to lamins in the inner nuclear membrane, 
which directly interact with chromatin. Interaction of LINC with actin 
and vimentin filaments establishes a mechanical axis that transmits 
mechanical information from the cell surface to the nucleus. The pres-
ence of such an axis has been elegantly shown by pulling DNA out of 
the nucleus using micromanipulated microbeads that engaged cell 
surface integrins246. Mechanical deformation of the nucleus alters 
chromatin organization initially by softening the nuclear envelope 
to prevent DNA damage247,248. This is followed by conversion of DNA 
from a condensed to an opened state, which also allows access for 
gene transcription or DNA repair factors249. For instance, nuclear stress 
affects the transcriptional regulation of fibrotic genes of mesenchymal 
cells on stiff substrates and promotes myofibroblast activation in dif-
ferent cells and organs, which is in part due to the state of chromatin 
condensation, in addition to control by signalling as described in 
Fig. 2 (refs. 237,239). Conversely, some of these studies have shown 
that disrupting the LINC complex in MSCs grown on stiff substrates 
results in high DNA condensation and prevents myofibroblast activa-
tion238. Because local stretching of the nucleus of Chinese hamster 
ovary lineage cells rapidly enhances the expression of genes in the 
correspondingly strained DNA region249, it is tempting to speculate 
that similar mechanisms apply to the regulation of mechanically 
responsive myofibroblast genes.
Epigenetic control of myofibroblast persistence
Compared to mechanical stress, histone acetylation is a more widely 
studied epigenetic mechanism controlling chromatin density, that 
is, accessibility of DNA regions for the binding of transcription fac-
tors. Although histone acetylation marks are typically associated with 
active gene transcription225, their role in controlling myofibroblast 
persistence is versatile. Reducing histone acetylation by inhibiting 
p300 (ref. 250) or increasing histone acetylation using inhibitors of 
HDACs251 both attenuate the expression of myofibroblast markers and 
fibrosis in animal models of cardiac injury. Consistently, skin biopsies 
and explanted dermal fibroblasts from patients with scleroderma show 
higher levels of the HAT p300 compared to fibroblasts from healthy 
individuals252. The presence and absence of histone acetylation marks 
can change the expression of fibrosis effector genes directly or indi-
rectly by modulating the expression of regulators of fibrosis (Fig. 3). 
One example of direct regulation is the p300-mediated hyperacetyla-
tion of the histone H3 (H3K27), which can be pharmacologically inhib-
ited to reduce myofibroblast activation (expression of ACTA2, COL1A1, 
COL15A1, POSTN) of fibroblasts isolated from the fibrotic palm tissue 
of patients with Dupuytren disease253. Likewise, p300 HAT levels are 
elevated in the skin of patients with systemic sclerosis; experimen-
tally enhanced levels of p300 HAT are associated with hyperacetyla-
tion of histone H4 at promoter regions of COL1A2 (encoding collagen 
type I, α2) in scleroderma fibroblasts, which could be blocked using 
selective inhibitors of p300 HAT252. The same study showed that TGFβ 
is a key inducer of EP300 (encoding p300) expression by enhancing 
binding of the transcription factor early growth response protein 1 
(Egr1) to the EP300 promoter252. Conversely, H3K27 de-acetylation 
at promoter regions of anti-fibrotic genes, such as PPARG (encoding 
peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-γ)254 by HDAC8 and PGC1A 
(encoding peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-γ co-activator 
1α)255 by HDAC7, leads to their reduced expression in TGFβ-activated 
lung fibroblasts. Indirect regulation of myofibroblast activation by 
histone acetylation marks was described for HDAC4, which mediates 
the elevated expression of ACTA2 (encoding αSMA) in TGFβ-treated 
cultured lung fibroblasts256. General inhibition of HDACs was shown 
to inhibit myofibroblast activation through reduced phosphorylation 
and nuclear translocation of signal transducer and activator of tran-
scription 3 (STAT3) in renal interstitial fibroblasts in vivo257. It seems 
that cytoplasmic–nuclear translocation of HDAC4 during myofibro-
blast activation is also driven by mechanical stress: mouse embryonic 
fibroblasts grown on stiff substrates (100 kPa) showed higher nuclear 
levels of HDAC4 and higher HDAC4 expression than fibroblasts grown 
on soft substrates (0.5 kPa)228. Analogously, stiff (25.6 kPa) but not soft 
Fig. 3 | Nuclear mechanotransduction and myofibroblast memory. a, The 
integrin-containing focal adhesions of fibroblasts adhering to soft extracellular 
matrix (ECM) are immature, that is, they lack molecular components that could 
facilitate the organization of cytoskeletal actin microfilaments into stress fibres. 
Such relaxed fibroblasts are also characterized by a low degree of polymerization 
of the intermediate filament vimentin, low lamin A to lamin C ratios in the inner 
nuclear membrane, and overall condensed chromatin. b, A stiff ECM provides 
high resistance, which enables the maturation of focal adhesions and promotes 
signalling processes that result in actin polymerization from globular (G-actin) 
to filamentous (F-actin) actin and the formation of stress fibres and, thus, lead to 
higher intracellular stress. F-actin stress fibres can establish a direct mechanical 
connection between ECM adhesions and chromatin in the cell nucleus by 
binding to the nuclear envelope spectrin repeat protein 1 (nesprin 1)  
and nesprin 2. Nesprins, in turn, bind to Sad1p and UNC-84 homology (SUN) 
proteins that span the nuclear membrane and, in the inner nuclear membrane, 
interact with lamin A, which attaches to chromatin. The complex of SUN 
proteins and nesprins in the nuclear outer membrane is called linker of 
nucleoskeleton and cytoskeleton (LINC) complex. Analogous to the connection 
of nesprin 1 and nesprin 2 with F-actin, vimentin filaments connect to nesprin 3.  
In both cases, transmission of stress from cytoskeletal filaments to the LINC 
complex forces opening of chromatin, thereby changing DNA accessibility 
for transcriptional and epigenetic regulations. The lamin A to lamin C ratio 
determines the stiffness of the nucleus and, thus, resistance to deformation. 
c, Nuclei of fibroblasts exposed to soft environments exhibit densely packed 
chromatin, high levels of repressive histone methylation, low levels of activating 
histone methylation, low histone acetylation and low DNA methylation. The 
implications of mechanically regulated epigenetic modifications are just 
beginning to be understood. d, Conversely, nuclei of myofibroblasts in stiff 
environments display a less compact chromatin structure, with low levels of 
repressive histone methylation but high activating histone methylation, histone 
acetylation, and DNA methylation levels. e, Epigenetic modification of the 
promoter regions of pro-fibrotic and anti-fibrotic genes by various epigenetics-
modifying enzymes drive persistent myofibroblast activation and fibrosis. 
Enzymes, such as ASH1, the methyltransferase (MET) COMPASS complex, mixed-
lineage leukaemia protein 1 (MLL1) and p300, place transcription-activating 
histone methylation and acetylation marks that promote the expression of pro-
fibrotic genes. Conversely, anti-fibrotic promoter regions are characterized by 
the removal of active histone acetylation marks by histone deacetylases (HDACs; 
for example, HDAC8) and the addition of repressive histone methylation 
marks by enzymes such as EZH2 and EHMT2. DNA methylation (5mC) by DNA 
methyltransferases (DNMTs) is associated with suppression of the expression 
of both pro-fibrotic genes (Acta2, Col1a1, Col2a1, Tgfb1, Timp1) and anti-fibrotic 
genes (Pparg, Pgc1a, FLIT, Socs3, Dkk1, Sfrp1, Thy1, Ptger2, Cox2, P14arf). CBX5, 
chromobox protein homologue 5.

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
632
Review article
culture environments (0.4 kPa) promoted the phosphorylation and 
subsequent nuclear translocation of the HAT p300 through enhanced 
AKT-dependent signalling during myofibroblast activation of human 
hepatic stellate cells (HSCs)258.
Histone methylations can either enhance or silence gene tran-
scription, depending on the location of the methyl mark in specific 
lysine residues of the histone tail. In general, histone 3 lysine 9 tri-
methylation (H3K9me3) and H3K27me3 are repressive methylation 
marks, whereas H3K4me3, H3K36me2 or H3K3me3, and H3K79me3 
activate gene transcription226. One of the few examples of inhibition 
of myofibroblast activation by repressive histone methylation of pro-
fibrotic genes is the silencing of the transcription factor Fos-related 
antigen 2 (Fra2) by H3K27me3 (ref. 259). However, in most cases, myofi-
broblast activation is achieved by activating histone methylation 
marks in pro-fibrotic and ECM-related genes260–262. Consequently, 
activated myofibroblasts isolated from fibrotic rat livers exhibit higher 
global levels of active histone methylation marks (H3K4me2 and/or 
H3K4me3) and lower levels of repressive histone marks (H3K9me3 
and H3K27me2) compared to quiescent HSCs from healthy liver260. 
These methylation changes are accompanied by enhanced expres-
sion of three H3K4-specific METs: mixed-lineage leukaemia protein 5 
(MLL5; also known as KMT2E), histone-lysine N-methyltransferase 
SETD1A and ASH1 (ref. 260). ASH1 is recruited to the proximal promot-
ers of Acta2, Timp1 (encoding metalloproteinase inhibitor 1), Col1a1, 
and Tgfb1 and enhances their expression by conferring the active 
histone mark H3K4me3 (ref. 260). Activating H3K4me3 or H3K4me2 
marks in the promoter regions of Col1a1, Col1a2 and Acta2 are further 
enriched by the MET complex COMPASS in TGFβ-activated HSCs261. 
Likewise, enrichment of MLL1 binding to the promoters of ECM-related 
genes results in H3K4 trimethylation and drives HSC–myofibroblast 
activation in experimental liver fibrosis262.
In some cases, myofibroblast activation is also achieved by repres-
sive histone methylation of the promoters or exons of genes that 
encode anti-fibrotic factors such as PPARγ (Fig. 3e). For instance, the 
MET EZH2 methylates H3K27 and thereby forms a repressive structure 
in regions downstream of the Pparg exon as shown in experimental 
models of induced liver fibrosis263. Likewise, experimentally knock-
ing down the histone-lysine demethylase 3A (KDM3A; also known as 
JMJD1A or JHDM2A) allowed repressive H3K9me2 to accumulate in the 
Pparg gene promoter, thereby enhancing myofibroblast activation of 
HSCs in culture and in a rat model of induced liver fibrosis264. Myofi-
broblast activation of rat renal fibroblasts is promoted by repressive 
H3K9 methylation of regulatory gene regions of the TGFβ inhibitor 
Klotho by the histone-lysine N-methyltransferase EHMT2 (also known 
as MET G9). Consistently, high levels of EHMT2 and H3K9me1 and low 
levels of Klotho have been measured in kidney biopsies of patients with 
tubulointerstitial fibrosis265. EHMT2 is also involved in lung myofibro-
blast activation by forming a complex with the epigenetic repressor 
chromobox protein homologue 5 (CBX5) to repress the promoter of the 
anti-fibrotic gene PGC1a in H3K9me236. The expression of EHMT2 itself 
is regulated through TGFβ1–SMAD3 signalling in renal fibroblasts265, 
similar to TGFβ1-induced expression of the METs SET7 and SET9 in 
myofibroblast-activated mesangial cells265.
There are some indications for mechanical control of MET activi-
ties through MRTFA: the presence of MRTFA binding sites in the pro-
moters of some pro-fibrotic genes, such as ACTA2, led to the discovery 
that MRTFA recruits METs (for example, COMPASS) in TGFβ-treated 
HSCs and, consequently, that depletion of MRTFA led to reduced 
H3K4 methylation and reduced myofibroblast activation261. Further 
complexity is added by the functions of histone methylations that are 
beyond conventional gene regulation. For instance, global H3K9me3-
marked heterochromatin was recently shown to protect the DNA of 
epithelial cells from mechanically induced damage248. It is tempting 
to speculate that myofibroblasts might use epigenetic modifications to 
globally protect their genome from the high stresses and strains arising 
during tissue remodelling but this is yet to be confirmed.
Control of myofibroblast persistence by DNA methylation
In addition to histone modifications, DNA methylation is one of the 
most powerful epigenetic drivers of organ fibrosis, where persistent 
myofibroblasts often exhibit global DNA hypermethylation266,267. DNA 
methylation is performed by DNA methyltransferases (DNMTs), which 
add methyl groups to cytosines in CpG islands (Fig. 3c–e); thus, DNA 
methylation in myofibroblasts is controlled through regulation of 
DNMT expression levels. DNMT1 and DNMT3a are upregulated in scle-
roderma skin and induced by pro-fibrotic TGFβ in dermal fibroblasts268. 
In a process mediated by hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF1α), DNMT1 
and DNMT3b are upregulated during cardiac fibrosis, which can be 
mitigated by knockdown of DNMT3b269. DNMT1 is also highly expressed 
in lung and kidney fibrosis, where it is a hallmark of myofibroblast 
persistence270,271. The DNMT inhibitor 5-azacytidine reduces fibrosis 
in various animal models268,269,272. Because DNA methylation generally 
promotes gene silencing by creating binding sites for repressor proteins 
in gene promoters225, myofibroblast activation from mesenchymal 
precursor cells is typically achieved by hypermethylation and thus by 
silencing of fibrosis-suppressing genes. Examples of DNA methylation-
regulated anti-fibrotic genes are FLI1, Socs3, Dkk1, SFRP1 (ref. 273), Thy1 
(ref. 274), PTGER2 (encoding prostaglandin E receptor 2 (ref. 275)) and 
Ptgs2 (encoding cyclooxygenase 2)276. Hypermethylation can further 
render myofibroblasts resistant to apoptosis through the silencing 
of P14ARF, which arrests the cell cycle and promotes myofibroblast 
persistence in fibrotic lungs277. One exciting option to therapeutically 
control myofibroblast fate related to DNA methylation is the use of 
inhibitors directed against specific epigenetic regulators, for instance, 
members of the methyl-CpG-binding domain (MBD) family and the 
bromodomain and extra-terminal domain (BET) families of epigenetic 
readers263,278 (Supplementary Box S4).
In addition to the hypermethylation of myofibroblast-suppressing 
genes, myofibroblast-associated genes have been reported to be hypo-
methylated in fibrotic conditions, most notably for Acta2. Reducing 
DNMT activity and thereby DNA methylation of the Acta2 promoter 
was shown to result in lung fibroblast activation279. However, rather 
than serving as a mechanism regulating fibroblast–myofibroblast 
activation, Acta2 hypomethylation is thought to prevent αSMA expres-
sion in non-mesenchymal cells, such as alveolar epithelial type II cells 
in the lungs279. Nevertheless, DNA hypomethylation was reported to 
support the acquisition of mesenchymal features in cultured cardiac 
endothelial cells after treatment with TGFβ1 and in experimental heart 
fibrosis271,280.
In contrast to the emerging connection between nuclear mechan-
ics, chromatin condensation and histone modifications, it is still 
unclear whether and how mechanical stress can regulate DNA meth-
ylation. In liver fibrosis, expression of the nuclear membrane compo-
nent SUN2 is downregulated by DNMT3b-mediated hypermethylation 
with adverse effects on genome stability, leading to pro-fibrotic 
effects281. ECM stiffness was shown to co-regulate DNMT1 activity 
together with myofibroblast-related phenotypes of SMCs282. Further-
more, the DNMT1 promoter contains binding motifs for MRTFA283, 

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology | Volume 25 | August 2024 | 617–638
633
Review article
and YAP1 was shown to regulate DNMT1 expression in liver cancer 
cells284, all suggesting potential regulation of DNA methylation by 
mechanical factors.
Conclusions and perspectives
Here, we reviewed the biological mechanisms of myofibroblast activa-
tion focusing on the impact of the mechanical environment and signal 
mechanotransduction. The involved mechanisms and factors present 
potential targets for therapeutic intervention to block myofibroblast 
formation and/or persistence. Indeed, some of these conceptual 
targets have progressed into clinical trials such as ECM-crosslinking 
enzymes, contraction mediators and specific integrins. We refer to 
recent reviews that provide clinically inclined scientists — or patients, 
for that matter — with the translational aspects of anti-myofibroblast 
and anti-fibrotic strategies13,14,285,286.
Although central to the physiological and pathological tissue 
repair process, myofibroblasts do not act in isolation but in concert 
with multiple other cell types. The role of fibroblast and myofibro-
blast communication with innate and adaptive immune cells, epi-
thelium cells, and vascular cells in driving tissue repair and fibrosis 
has been reviewed elsewhere119,285,287. Accumulating evidence shows 
that normal and fibrotic repair engage very similar if not identical 
cell activation states. However, whether scar tissue accumulates or 
not depends on the spatial and temporal circumstances under which 
these activation states occur288,289. One example of the importance of 
spatiotemporal coordination in wound environments is the crosstalk 
between fibroblastic cells and macrophages, which has (re-)gained 
increasing interest in the past years. Macrophage–myofibroblast 
crosstalk has been described for various fibrotic organs and is sug-
gested to promote fibrosis by locally regulating the activation state 
of fibroblastic cells290–295.
Macrophages, just like fibroblastic cells, acquire different func-
tional ‘polarization’ states over the course of tissue repair295. Macro­
phages with both pro-inflammatory or pro-fibrotic and pro-repair 
functions are crucial for the successful repair and scar resolution 
of injured tissues (Fig. 1a). An imbalance in these functions or mis-
timing of the associated macrophage polarization states can result 
in either non-healing wounds296,297 or fibrosis295,298,299. This is in part 
due to the impact that different macrophages have on the activation 
state of fibroblastic cells. For instance, experimental depletion of 
macrophages in healing mouse skin wounds stimulates the expan-
sion of a myofibroblast subpopulation that is associated with dermal 
repair300. Conventionally, macrophages are known to control fibro-
blast survival, proliferation, collagen production and myofibroblast 
activation through the paracrine secretion of cytokines85,294,295,301–304. 
More recently, direct contact has been shown to be critical for specific 
molecular signals to pass between macrophages and myofibroblasts. 
Our own studies support the notion that physical contact between 
fibroblasts and pro-fibrotic macrophages is maintained by hetero­
cellular junctions and creates a niche of active TGFβ1 that induces and 
maintains myofibroblasts in their highly contractile, αSMA-positive 
state305. We are only beginning to understand the molecular interac-
tions at play in such heterocellular niches, the study of which is an 
emerging field further driven by the power of spatial transcriptomics41. 
It is already clear that it will unlikely suffice to target single cell types 
in anti-fibrosis therapies without considering the spatiotemporal 
context of the condition.
Published online: 8 April 2024

===== Yu2019_FrontMolNeurosci_SCI+Transcriptome =====
ORIGINAL RESEARCH
published: 22 November 2019
doi: 10.3389/fnmol.2019.00287
Edited by:
George Smith,
Temple University, United States
Reviewed by:
Junfang Wu,
University of Maryland, Baltimore,
United States
Antje Kroner,
Medical College of Wisconsin,
United States
*Correspondence:
Xiaosong Gu
nervegu@ntu.edu.cn
†These authors have contributed
equally to this work
Received: 09 August 2019
Accepted: 12 November 2019
Published: 22 November 2019
Citation:
Yu B, Yao C, Wang Y, Mao S,
Wang Y, Wu R, Feng W, Chen Y,
Yang J, Xue C, Liu D, Ding F and
Gu X (2019) The Landscape of Gene
Expression and Molecular Regulation
Following Spinal Cord Hemisection
in Rats. Front. Mol. Neurosci. 12:287.
doi: 10.3389/fnmol.2019.00287
The Landscape of Gene Expression
and Molecular Regulation Following
Spinal Cord Hemisection in Rats
Bin Yu1,2†, Chun Yao1†, Yongjun Wang1†, Susu Mao1, Yaxian Wang1, Ronghua Wu1,
Wei Feng1, Yanping Chen1, Jian Yang1, Chengbin Xue1, Dong Liu1, Fei Ding1 and
Xiaosong Gu1,2*
1 Key Laboratory of Neuroregeneration of Jiangsu and Ministry of Education, Co-innovation Center of Neuroregeneration,
Nantong University, Nantong, China, 2 Jiangsu Clinical Medicine Center of Tissue Engineering and Nerve Injury Repair,
Afﬁliated Hospital of Nantong University, Nantong University, Nantong, China
Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a challenging clinical problem worldwide. The cellular
state and molecular expression in spinal cord tissue after injury are extremely
complex and closely related to functional recovery. However, the spatial and temporal
changes of gene expression and regulation in various cell types after SCI are
still unclear. Here, we collected the rostral and caudal regions to the lesion at
11 time points over a period of 28 days after rat hemisection SCI. Combining
whole-transcriptome sequencing and bioinformatic analysis, we identiﬁed differentially
expressed genes (DEGs) between spinal cord tissue from injured and sham-operated
animals. Signiﬁcantly altered biological processes were enriched from DEGs in
astrocytes, microglia, oligodendrocytes, immune cells, and vascular systems after SCI.
We then identiﬁed dynamic trends in these processes using the average expression
proﬁles of DEGs. Gene expression and regulatory networks for selected biological
processes were also constructed to illustrate the complicate difference between rostral
and caudal tissues. Finally, we validated the expressions of some key genes from these
networks, including α-synuclein, heme oxygenase 1, bone morphogenetic protein 2,
activating transcription factor 3, and leukemia inhibitory factor. Collectively, we provided
a comprehensive network of gene expression and regulation to shed light on the
molecular characteristics of critical biological processes that occur after SCI, which will
broaden the understanding of SCI and facilitate clinical therapeutics for SCI.
Keywords: spinal cord injury, RNA-sequencing, microenvironment, astrocyte, microglia, oligodendrocyte,
vasculation
INTRODUCTION
Spinal cord injury (SCI) causes devastating neurological deﬁcits and disability with 10s of 1000s
of new cases around the world every year (Singh et al., 2014). Despite considerable global research
eﬀorts, there are currently no eﬀective and reliable clinical treatments for patients with SCI (Silva
et al., 2014). In addition, outcomes from standard surgical and pharmacological interventions
are inconsistent.
Permanent functional deﬁcits after SCI mainly arise from the weak regeneration capacity of
neurons and the inhibitory environment around the injury site (Du et al., 2015). The primary
(mechanical) injury—which usually involves contusion and compression by blunt force—results
Frontiers in Molecular Neuroscience | www.frontiersin.org
1
November 2019 | Volume 12 | Article 287

Yu et al.
Regulation Networks in Spinal Cord Injury
in damage to neurons, axons, and glia at the site of the injury.
Over the following days to weeks, the mechanical injury leads
to a cascade of biological events within and around the primary
lesion, which are collectively referred to as secondary injury.
These events include ischemia, edema, inﬂammation, cellular
necrosis, and vascular changes (Silva et al., 2014). Neuronal cell
death contributes to SCI-induced neurological deﬁcits; however,
much of this is delayed cell death as a result of the secondary
injuries rather than direct mechanical damage (Liu et al., 2015).
Numerous biochemical mechanisms have been proposed to
explain the progressive lesions in spinal cord tissue after SCI,
including apoptosis of neurons and glial cells, inﬂammatory
responses, vascular changes, free radical formation, lipid
peroxidation, changes in ionic balance, glutamate excitotoxicity,
and glial scar formation (Silva et al., 2014). With respect to
the latter, astrocytes transform into a reactive state after SCI,
becoming hypertrophic and proliferative, and form a border
between the injury site and surrounding tissues. Eventually the
reactive astrocytes, microglia, macrophages, and extracellular
matrix molecules form a glial scar, which acts as a physical barrier
to re-growing axons.
Because of the many processes involved in secondary injuries,
an in-depth exploration of the temporal and spatial changes in
gene expression and molecular regulation following SCI is of
the utmost importance – an understanding of these processes
would facilitate the development of novel and eﬀective therapies
to minimize the extent of the lesion and improve recovery.
A number of previous studies have investigated the changes in
molecules, cells, and tissues that occur after SCI (Nishimura et al.,
2014; Duan et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2017).
Here, we used a partial spinal transection (hemisection),
which is a commonly used animal model of SCI (Kozuka
et al., 2016; Martini et al., 2016), and compared changes
in the rostral (R) and caudal (C) regions of the injury
site with each other and with sham-injured animals over a
period of 28 days after injury. We analyzed the dynamic
trends in gene expression and the related biological processes
using ingenuity pathway analysis (IPA). The spatial and
temporal characteristics of key genes in speciﬁc cell types
and certain biological processes after SCI were determined
with subsequent immunoﬂuorescent veriﬁcation. Overall, our
study provides new insights into the cellular and molecular
processes after SCI.
RESULTS
Differential Gene Expression in Rostral
and Caudal Sites Following Spinal Cord
Injury
We performed a spinal cord hemisection in rats and collected
two 5-mm-long segments from the injury site, one in the rostral
(R) direction from the injury and one in the caudal (C) direction
(Supplementary Figure S1a). The segments were collected from
six rats at each time point after SCI or sham surgery (0, 0.5,
3, 6, 12 h and 1, 3, 7, 14, 21, 28 days) and were subsequently
prepared for RNA extraction and RNA sequencing. The map-to-
gene and map-to-genome data suggested good sequence quality
(Supplementary Table S1).
By comparing the injured (R and C) groups with the sham
group (S), we identiﬁed diﬀerentially expressed genes (DEGs)
for both the rostral and caudal regions after SCI. As shown in
Supplementary Figure S1b, during the initial period after SCI
(0–3 h), there were no signiﬁcant diﬀerences in gene expression
between the R, C, and S groups. In the early phase (6 h –
1 day), the number of DEGs increased to 100s in the R and C
regions, with no notable diﬀerences between these groups. In the
middle and late periods (3–28 days), the number of DEGs rapidly
increased to 1000s for both the R and C regions. However, the
number of DEGs was markedly smaller in the C region than
in the R region.
Molecular Regulation of Astrocyte
Responses
Approximately 90% of cells in the CNS are glia, and the majority
of these are astrocytes (Ge et al., 2007). After SCI, astrocytes
become reactive, with enlarged soma and increased glial ﬁbrillary
acidic protein (GFAP) expression. This is referred to as the
hypertrophic phase. Hyperplastic responses are followed by an
increase in the number of astrocytes, which contributes to glial
scar formation (Bhalala et al., 2012).
We used GFAP as the marker of astrocyte, as shown in
Figure 1A, astrocytes showed signiﬁcantly larger cell bodies,
hypertrophy of processes, and robust GFAP expression following
SCI, indicating their reactive state. We then performed an
IPA analysis to identify the DEGs involved in astrocytic
biological processes (Figure 1B). Most of these DEGs were
enriched in processes involved in astrocyte activation and
diﬀerentiation. We analyzed the average expression proﬁles of
the DEGs involved in vital astrocytic activities to obtain an
overall view of the time-dependent changes in these processes.
Six major biological processes of astrocytes (activation of
astrocyte, diﬀerentiation of astrocyte, astrocytosis, development
of astrocyte, proliferation of astrocyte, and survival of astrocyte)
were shown, and we calculated the Z-scores for the expression
trends of genes involved in these biological processes (Figure 1C).
Compared with the sham group, astrocytes underwent survival,
proliferation, activation, and astrocytosis sequentially after SCI,
consistent with previous reports.
We then focused on astrocyte activation, which is an
important biological process that involves changes in cell
morphology and molecular expression in response to SCI.
By IPA, we created a network map of gene expression and
regulation during astrocyte activation. It showed that, after SCI,
an increasing number of genes involved in astrocyte activation
were upregulated in R vs. S until 7 days, although a set of
genes was also downregulated from 1 day (Figure 1D and
Supplementary Table S2). In the C vs. S group, the trends in gene
expression were similar to those in R vs. S, except that the number
of downregulated genes was smaller.
We selected six distinct changed genes for qRT-PCR validation
(Figure 1E). Matrix metallopeptidase 9 (MMP9) and serpin
Frontiers in Molecular Neuroscience | www.frontiersin.org

family E member 1 (SERPINE1) reached the highest levels of
expression at 12 h. Interleukin 1 beta (IL1b) was highly expressed
from 3 h after SCI and returned to sham levels after 3 days. Toll-
like receptor 2 (TLR2) and CCAAT enhancer binding protein
beta (CEBPB) were also highly expressed following SCI. The
expression of Synuclein Alpha (SNCA) decreased after injury,
and this was reﬂected also in the immunoﬂuorescence results
(Figure 1F). SNCA is a pre-synaptic protein that self-associates
Frontiers in Molecular Neuroscience | www.frontiersin.org
3
November 2019 | Volume 12 | Article 287

Yu et al.
Regulation Networks in Spinal Cord Injury
into toxic oligomers (Burre, 2015). Our results indicate that
SNCA may inhibit the activation of astrocytes in response to SCI.
Molecular Regulation of Microglial
Responses
The activation of microglia is a prominent hallmark of
neuropathology following SCI, and is regulated by a variety of
cytokines and chemokines. We carried out immunoﬂuorescence
staining for ionized calcium-binding adapter molecule 1 (IBA1),
a marker of microglia and macrophages (which act to remove
dead cells and cell debris). The staining showed that post-
traumatic accumulation of microglia or invading macrophages
was signiﬁcantly increased at 3 days and the remained elevated
up to 28 days after SCI (Figure 2A). The DEGs involved in
microglia biological processes were enriched in activation of
microglia (Figure 2B). The expression trends of DEGs in some
enriched microglia biological processes were also shown in
Figure 2C. The transcriptional proﬁles of the DEGs indicated a
dynamic regulation of activated microglia, temporally controlled
by multiple cytokines, chemokines, cell surface molecules,
and proteases (Figures 2C,D and Supplementary Table S3).
We observed that IL-6 was upregulated 3–12 h after SCI
(Figures 2D,E). Cytochrome b-245 beta chain (CYBB), an
enzyme system that generates reactive oxygen species (ROS)
in microglia, was markedly upregulated following SCI. Erb-b2
receptor tyrosine kinase 2 (ERBB2) and transforming growth
factor beta 1 (TGFB1) showed a slight increase. However,
expression of anti-inﬂammatory heme oxygenase 1 (HMOX1),
which is protective against oxidative stress in cells (Ghattas
et al., 2002), increased substantially after SCI (Figures 2D,E); this
supports the idea that activated microglia are both deleterious
and beneﬁcial to the functional outcome of the injured CNS.
Immunoﬂuorescence results conﬁrmed the HMOX1 expression
changes in microglia (Figure 2F).
Molecular Regulation of Oligodendrocyte
Responses
Oligodendrocytes are a fundamental and unique cell type in
the central nervous system and are responsible for myelinating
axons (Kopp and Mendell, 2018). We used Oligo2 as a marker
of oligodendrocytes. Immunoﬂuorescence data showed a robust
increase in oligodendrocytes at 3 days post-SCI, but the number
of oligodendrocytes subsequently declined (Figure 3A). There
seemed to be no notable diﬀerences in Oligo2 expression between
the R and C sites.
Ingenuity
pathway
analysis
analysis
suggests
that
the
DEGs associated with oligodendrocytes are mainly involved
in oligodendrocyte diﬀerentiation, development, apoptosis,
and survival (Figure 3B). We also calculated the Z-scores for
the expression trends of genes involved in these biological
processes (Figure 3C). The results showed that genes related
to
oligodendrocyte
apoptosis,
survival,
development,
and
diﬀerentiation were upregulated prior to 1 day post-SCI and were
downregulated at subsequent time points. Another interesting
ﬁnding is that there was an upregulation of oligodendrocyte
precursor cell diﬀerentiation in the S group from 3 h to 3 days,
whereas this increase was delayed at following times in the
R and C regions.
We then focused on oligodendrocyte diﬀerentiation, and
created a network map of gene expression and regulation during
this process (Figure 3D and Supplementary Table S4). From
this, we selected distinct changed genes bone morphogenetic
protein 2 (BMP2), hepatocyte growth factor (HGF), tumor
protein p53 (TP53), leucine rich repeat and Ig domain containing
1 (LINGO1), tenascin C (TNC), and versican (VCAN) for qRT-
PCR validation (Figure 3E). BMP signaling has been reported
to suppress oligodendrocyte development (Cheng et al., 2007).
In addition, the immunoﬂuorescence results conﬁrmed the
changes in expression of the BMP2,a signaling molecule that
regulates oligodendrogenesis (Figure 3F). These results indicate
that BMP2 may play an important role in oligodendrocytes
diﬀerentiation after SCI.
Molecular and Cellular Immune
Responses to Spinal Cord Injury
Inﬂammation provoked by SCI can result in further tissue
damage and neurodegeneration, hindering functional recovery.
Z-score expression trends suggested that SCI triggers a sequential
recruitment of neutrophils, monocytes, and lymphocytes to the
lesion site (Figure 4A). Analysis of the transcriptional proﬁles
indicated that cytokine IL-1β and other six chemokines (CCL3,
CCL4, CCL5, CCL7, CXCL2, and CXCL3), were upregulated
from 0.5 h post-SCI (Supplementary Figure S2c). It is interesting
to
note
that
several
inﬂammation-related
transcriptional
factors, such as the members of the CCAAT/enhancer-binding
protein (CEBP) family, ATF3, and RUNX proteins, were
markedly upregulated and participate in the progression of
the inﬂammatory responses (Supplementary Figures S2a,b).
The functions of these transcriptional factors are deeply woven
into immune processes; these transcriptional factors therefore
have substantial potential to resolve inﬂammation in the
injured spinal cord.
Next, we selected the distinct changed transcriptional factors
ATF3 and FOS for qRT-PCR validation. The results show that
the expression of both ATF3 and FOS increased following SCI,
and that the elevated level of ATF3 was maintained until 28 days
post-injury (Figure 4B). Immunostaining indicated that ATF3
was expressed in IBA1 positive cells (Figure 4C).
Vascular System After Spinal Cord Injury
We used CD34 as a marker for blood vessels to detect vascular
changes after SCI. The density of blood vessels was greater at
3 days after SCI than at 0 day, then decreased again by 14 days
(Figure 5A). However, at 28 days, there appeared to be a slight
increase in blood vessel density. Throughout, the density and
intensity of blood vessels were weaker at the R site than at
the C site. The IPA analysis showed that the DEGs associated
with vascular endothelial cells (VECs) were mainly enriched in
VEC migration, proliferation, adhesion, apoptosis, and activation
pathways (Supplementary Figure S3a). We calculated the
dynamic changes in these biological processes (Supplementary
Figure S3b). In addition to these VEC processes, we also

detected hypoxia, sprouting angiogenesis, and blood-vessel
remodeling, which are three key events during angiogenesis
after nerve injury. Dynamic networks for gene expression and
regulation were mapped for hypoxia, sprouting angiogenesis,
blood-vessel remodeling and VEC activation (Figures 5B–D and
Supplementary Figure S3c, Tables S5–S7). We then performed
qRT-PCR to validate several key genes involved in these
processes, including FGF9, LIF, THBS1, RUNX2, XDH, PLAUR,
FLNA, and CD14 (Figure 5E). Immunoﬂuorescence results
conﬁrmed changes in LIF expression in blood vessels (Figure 5F).
DISCUSSION
Spinal cord injury not only causes direct neurological dysfunction
but is also accompanied by a cascade of pathophysiological

oligodendrocytes. (D) Network maps of the changes in gene expression and regulation related to oligodendrocyte differentiation following SCI. (E) qRT-PCR

(red) expressing BMP2 are shown in yellow. Scale bar: 10 µm.
events, such as inﬂammation, demyelination, and loss of blood
supply, that contribute to a hostile microenvironment that
inhibits nerve regeneration (Sinescu et al., 2010). Considering
the poor outcome for functional recovery after SCI, it is
crucial to obtain a thorough understanding of gene expression
and molecular modulation following SCI. In this study, we

hemisected the spinal cord in rats and measured the dynamic
alterations in gene expression in the spinal cord regions
R and C to the injury site, over 28 days after SCI. We
examined the glial, immune, and vascular systems in detail.
We identiﬁed the spatial and temporal characteristics of several
pivotal genes in speciﬁc cell types, thus providing a global
view of gene expression and molecular regulation following
SCI (Figure 6).

FIGURE 5 | Vascular system after SCI. (A) Pathological changes in vascular system following SCI as detected by CD34 antibody (green). Partial areas in the regions
