Fig. 3 | The mediators of fibroblast activation. a, Soluble factors such as Shh, 
PDGF, FGF2, Ang II, TGFβ and Wnts are often upregulated in fibrotic organs and 
have important roles in triggering fibroblast activation. TGFβ, Wnt and Ang II 
sequentially induce the expression of each other and constitute a core signalling 
network known as the TWA cycle, which drives fibroblast activation and organ 
fibrosis in a wide variety of disease settings. b, Extracellular vesicles have a critical 
role in fibroblast activation and organ fibrosis. In the kidney, tubular epithelial 
cells, monocytes, macrophages, podocytes and endothelial cells release 
numerous extracellular vesicles in response to injurious stimuli. Extracellular 
vesicles transport a wide variety of endogenous profibrotic factors to activated 
fibroblast precursors, including DNA, messenger RNA (mRNA), microRNAs 
(miRNAs), long non-coding RNA (lncRNA), circular RNA (circRNA), proteins, 
lipids, amino acids and metabolites, and thereby contribute to fibroblast 
activation. c, Fibroblast activation is also regulated by matricellular proteins such 
as TNC, FBN1, VTN, SMOC2, CTGF and WISP, via various mechanisms, including 
recruiting soluble factors and binding to and activating cell surface receptors. 
d, Fibroblast activation is also facilitated by mechanical cues. Activated 
fibroblast precursors sense extracellular matrix (ECM) stiffness through ECM 
receptors and stretch-activated ion channels (SACs), resulting in the activation 
of various biochemical and biomechanical pathways. These pathways facilitate 
the nuclear translocation of profibrotic transcription factors such as Smad2/3, 
YAP/TAZ and MRTFA, thereby activating the expression of fibrotic genes.  
dsDNA, double-stranded DNA; ssDNA, single-stranded DNA; tRNA, transfer RNA.

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
620
Review article
Notch signalling also has an instrumental role in regulating fibro-
blast activation during organ fibrosis104,105. The Notch pathway involves 
interactions between Notch receptors and their ligands, which are 
typically transmembrane proteins of the Jagged (JAG1, JAG2) and 
Delta-like families. Notch signalling is initiated by the binding of a 
Notch receptor on one cell to a ligand on an adjacent cell, which trig-
gers a series of proteolytic cleavages and modifications of the Notch 
receptor. These changes result in release of the Notch intracellular 
domain from the plasma membrane. The Notch intracellular domain 
then translocates into the nucleus, where it forms a complex with 
transcription factors and activates the expression of its target genes. 
JAG–NOTCH signalling is activated in damaged organs after tissue 
injury, and tubule-specific deletion of JAG1 or NOTCH2 protected 
against kidney fibrosis in mice104. By contrast, in another mouse model, 
loss of Homeobox protein Hox-A5 (HOXA5) owing to hypermethylation 
led to JAG1 expression and NOTCH signalling and thereby contributed 
to kidney fibrosis105.
Vasoconstrictor peptides. Angiotensin II (Ang II), the main effec-
tor of the RAAS, has a central role in regulating blood pressure and 
fluid balance, but has also been implicated in fibroblast activation 
and the development of organ fibrosis106. When tissues are injured or 
undergo stress, Ang II can be locally produced and activate resident 
fibroblasts via binding to Ang II type 1 receptor (AT1) expressed on their 
surface. This activation causes the resident fibroblasts to transform 
into matrix-producing activated fibroblasts and promotes fibroblast 
proliferation, leading to an increase in the number of activated fibro-
blasts in the affected tissue. Ang II can also induce activated fibroblasts 
to produce and secrete ECM components as well as promote their 
migration to the site of tissue injury or remodelling. In addition, Ang II 
stimulates the production of various profibrotic factors, including 
TGFβ and PDGF107, that promote the proliferation and activation of 
fibroblasts, further driving the fibrotic process. Ang II also promotes 
renal inflammation by stimulating the release of inflammatory 
cytokines and chemokines and induces vasoconstriction of renal blood 
vessels. The resulting reduction in blood flow and oxygen delivery 
could lead to tissue hypoxia, which triggers the generation of reactive 
oxygen species, leading to oxidative stress, which promotes fibroblast 
activation and the production of ECM proteins, thereby contributing 
to organ fibrosis108.

The TWA cycle. Soluble factors that participate in regulating fibroblast 
activation are not present in isolation within injured organs. In the vast 
majority of chronic fibrotic diseases, multiple profibrotic signalling 
pathways are often simultaneously activated. Many of these pathways 
mutually stimulate and crosstalk, and together they constitute a core 
signalling network that promotes fibroblast activation.
Among profibrotic factors, TGFβ, Wnts and Ang II are of particular 
importance, not only because each of them can independently drive 
fibroblast activation but also because they can sequentially induce the 
expression and activation of each other and other key signalling path-
ways. For example, TGFβ can activate Wnt–β-catenin signalling via mul-
tiple mechanisms, such as inducing the expression of Wnt ligands109, 
suppressing the expression of the Wnt antagonist Klotho110,111, liberating 
β-catenin by inducing MMP7-mediated E-cadherin degradation112, trans-
activating β-catenin via the CXCR4–Src–EGFR–GSK3β cascade113 or the 
MMP10–HB–EGF–EGFR–GSK3β cascade114, and stabilizing β-catenin 
mRNA via METTL3–IGF2BP3-mediated m6A methylation115,116. Similarly, 
activation of Wnt–β-catenin signalling promotes the induction of the 
RAAS components, angiotensinogen, renin, angiotensin-converting 
enzyme and AT1 receptor94, leading to increased generation and action 
of Ang II. Furthermore, Ang II can induce TGFβ expression117. TGFβ, Wnt 
and Ang II can thereby establish a vicious feedforward cycle, leading 
to a profound amplification of profibrotic signalling (Fig. 3a). This 
TGFβ–Wnt–Ang II core signalling network, which we have designated 
the TWA cycle, drives fibroblast activation and organ fibrosis in a wide 
variety of disease settings118.
Extracellular vesicles
Increasing evidence suggests that EVs have a central role in fibroblast 
activation and organ fibrosis11. Based on their size, biogenesis and 
release routes, EVs can be broadly divided into three main subgroups: 
exosomes, microvesicles and apoptotic bodies77. Exosomes are EVs 
of endosomal origin that are released into the extracellular space by 
fusion of multi-vesicular bodies (MVBs) with the plasma membrane. 
They range in size from 30 to 150 nm in diameter. Microvesicles are 
released by budding of the plasma membrane and range in size from 
50 nm to 1 µm, whereas apoptotic bodies are shed from the plasma 
membrane of apoptotic cells and range in size from 200 nm to 5 mm 
in diameter. In this Review, we use the term EVs to refer to exosomes 
and microvesicles (Fig. 3b).
Almost all parenchymal and inflammatory cells can release 
EVs under physiological and pathological conditions. In the kidney, 
the primary sources of EVs are tubular epithelial cells, monocytes, 
macrophages, podocytes and endothelial cells77,119. EVs transport a 
wide variety of endogenous substances between the EV donor and 
recipient cells, including DNA, mRNA, microRNAs, long non-coding 
RNA, circular RNA, proteins, lipids, amino acids and metabolites77. 
This transport enables EVs to regulate recipient cells in close proxim-
ity or over a long distance from the donor cell, without the need for 
direct cell-to-cell contact. In addition, the cargo of the EV is usually 
enriched in bioactive molecules from the donor cell, enabling robust 
and efficient delivery of these molecules to recipient cells.
EVs can be taken up by recipient cells through direct fusion, endo-
cytosis or receptor-mediated processes. In contrast to soluble factors, 
which bind to specific receptors on target cells, EVs do not require cog-
nate receptors on recipient cells for intercellular signalling because they 
can deliver ligands, receptors and intracellular mediators to the recipi-
ent cell. This property makes EVs-mediated signalling more efficient 
than signalling via soluble factors. Studies suggest that most EVs can 
traverse many biological barriers, including the glomerular basement 
membrane, to complete the transfer of their contents, supporting the 
idea that EVs act as integral messengers for intercellular communication.
Increasing evidence suggests that EVs have important roles in 
mediating fibroblast activation and organ fibrogenesis. For example, 
EVs produced by damaged or stressed tubular epithelial cells in dis-
eased kidneys can deliver TGFβ1 mRNA119, Shh ligand11, miR-21 (ref. 120), 
and osteopontin121 to interstitial fibroblasts, to induce fibroblast acti-
vation, proliferation and ECM production. As EVs contain a diverse 
array of biomaterials, whether the delivery of a particular molecule or 
a combination of molecules is required or sufficient for EV-mediated 
fibroblast activation remains unknown.
Matricellular proteins
Fibroblast activation is also regulated by the local microenvironment 
in which the ECM and associated proteins constitute extracellular scaf-
folds. As ECM proteins are insoluble, non-diffusional and stationary 
in nature, their actions are restricted to a particular spatial location. 
Unlike soluble factors, ECM proteins regulate the activity and fate 
of cells in their vicinity via binding to cell membrane receptors such 
as integrins or recruiting and presenting soluble factors such as TGFβ 
and Wnt ligands, thereby controlling fibroblast activation in a spatially 
confined fashion (Fig. 3c).
Matricellular proteins are a group of structurally unrelated, 
ECM-associated glycoproteins that are often incorporated into the 
ECM network and have important regulatory functions, but are not 
required for maintenance of the structural integrity of the ECM scaffold. 
Several well-characterized matricellular proteins have been found to 
have important roles in fibroblast activation, including TNC10, fibrillin-1 
(FBN1)122, vitronectin (VTN)123, connective tissue growth factor (CTGF), 
Wnt1-inducible signalling pathway protein-1 (WISP1), periostin (POSTN), 
thrombospondin-1 (TSP1) and secreted modular calcium-binding 
protein 2 (SMOC2)124.
TNC is a hexameric glycoprotein that is expressed at very low 
levels in healthy adult tissues and rapidly upregulated in fibrotic dis-
orders of many organs124,125. TNC modulates cellular behaviours by 
interacting with cell surface receptors such as integrins126 and Toll-like 
receptor-4 (TLR4) and regulates various signalling pathways via the 
recruitment of soluble factors125. As TNC is induced early after injury 
and can recruit soluble factors, it has been proposed to function as an 
organizer that orchestrates the formation of the fibrogenic niche for 
fibroblast activation10,124.
FBN1 is a cysteine-rich, calcium-binding ECM glycoprotein that is 
found in elastic and non-elastic connective tissue. Serum FBN1 levels 
closely correlate with the severity and stage of CKD122 and FBN1 was 
significantly upregulated in decellularized kidney tissue scaffolds from 
fibrotic kidneys124. FBN1 binds directly to cell surface receptors, such as 
integrins, interacts with fibronectin and elastin, and anchors various 
cytokines such as TGFβ1 and bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) to 
regulate fibroblast activation122 and endothelial injury127.
VTN is a glycoprotein that is abundantly present in blood and the 
extracellular space and facilitates cell adhesion, proliferation, migra-
tion and tissue remodelling128. In fibrotic kidneys, VTN has been shown 
to be an integral component of the fibrogenic niche that mediates 
macrophage–fibroblast crosstalk and enhances fibroblast activation123.
CTGF, also known as cellular communication network factor 2 
(CCN2), is a matricellular protein of the CCN family of ECM-associated 
heparin-binding proteins. CTGF can interact with multiple soluble 
factors, including TGFβ1, and membrane receptors, including LRP6 

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
622
Review article
and integrins, to promote fibroblast activation, ECM accumulation 
and cell adhesion129,130.
WISP-1, also known as CCN4, is a member of the CTGF family and 
exhibits similar profibrotic functions to CTGF131. Other matricellular 
proteins, such as POSTN, TSP1 and SMOC2, also have crucial roles in 
fibroblast activation via various mechanisms, including recruiting 
soluble factors and binding to and activating cell surface receptors77.
Mechanical cues
Fibroblast activation is also facilitated by biomechanical cues such 
as ECM stiffness132. Tissue stiffness is determined by factors such as 
collagen content, cross-linking and overall structure. Excessive ECM 
deposition by activated fibroblasts results in increased tissue stiffness, 
which in turn potentiates fibroblast activation, creating a positive feed-
back loop21. Fibroblasts sense extracellular mechanical stimuli through 
ECM receptors, such as integrins, CD44, discoidin domain receptor 1 
(DDR1) and DDR2, and stretch-activated ion channels (SACs), such as 
transient receptor potential cation channel subfamily V member 4 
(TRPV4) and Piezo1 (refs. 21,133) (Fig. 3d).
Tissue stiffness can activate specific signalling pathways in 
fibroblasts, leading to changes in gene expression, cell proliferation, 
differentiation, migration and other cellular responses. Stiff ECM 
transmits contraction forces via integrins to the arginine–glycine–
aspartate (RGD)-binding domain in the latency-associated peptide 
(LAP) of latent TGFβ, leading to activation of TGFβ and subsequent 
downstream signalling134. Integrins also respond to direct mechanical 
forces by altering their conformation, forming adhesion complexes 
and interacting with myosin to modulate cellular processes21,35,134. 
Biochemical pathways such as TGFβ–Smad intersect with biome-
chanical pathways at multiple junctures and form an intricate sig-
nalling network, involving integrins, focal adhesion kinase (FAK), 
Rho-Rho-associated kinase (ROCK), myocardin-related transcrip-
tion factor A (MRTFA), Yes-associated protein (YAP) and transcrip-
tional co-activator with PDZ-binding motif (TAZ), that activates 
fibroblasts135. Enhanced mechanical strain can also activate SACs 
such as TRPV4, which in turn regulates fibroblast ECM remodelling by 
controlling Ca2+ influx and thereby promotes fibroblast activation21,133.
Studies of tissue stiffness signalling are ongoing and many 
details of the complex interplay between mechanical cues and cellu-
lar responses are yet to be unravelled. Elucidation of the molecular 
mechanisms that underlie tissue stiffness signalling will hopefully 
enable the identification of novel targets for therapeutic interven-
tion and improve understanding of how mechanical forces influence 
fibroblast activation and disease progression.
The niche for fibroblast activation
Fibrosis is typically not evenly distributed in organs with chronic 
fibrotic diseases, suggesting a role of the local microenvironment in 
activating fibroblasts during organ fibrogenesis. We proposed the con-
cept of the fibrogenic niche — a special extracellular microenvironment 
that promotes fibroblast activation and proliferation10,124. This concept 
has been accepted in the fields of liver and lung fibrosis62,136–138, high-
lighting the commonality of tissue fibrogenesis in different organs. We 
discussed the cellular and molecular composition and the mechanisms 
of action of the fibrogenic niche in detail in a previous Review124.
The key elements of the fibrogenic niche presumably com-
prise cellular components, such as various parenchymal cells and infil-
trated inflammatory cells, a specialized ECM network, and an assortment 
of soluble factors, EVs and metabolites124. During the evolution of organ 
fibrosis, ECM proteins undergo qualitative and quantitative changes, 
and dynamically regulate various signalling pathways in fibroblasts, 
thereby altering the phenotypes and fates of these cells139–141. The ECM 
network serves as the backbone of the fibrogenic niche, providing an 
architectural scaffold for surrounding cells, particularly fibroblasts. 
In many aspects, the relationship between the ECM and fibroblasts 
is reminiscent of the ‘soil and seeds’ hypothesis of metastasis, which 
suggests that cancer cells (“the seed”) only grow and spread in organs 
(“the soil”) that provide a favourable microenvironment for their survival 
and proliferation142. Similarly, the behaviours and fates of fibroblasts 
are controlled by their ECM microenvironment. As the ECM and its 
associated proteins are non-diffusional and stationary, their actions 
on fibroblast activation are restricted to a particular location, where 
excessive ECM proteins are deposited and fibrotic foci eventually form.
Our proteomics analysis of decellularized kidney tissue scaffolds 
revealed the landscape of ECM proteins in the fibrotic kidney143. These 
scaffolds retain the 3D architecture of the kidney in the absence of cells, 
EVs and soluble factors10,123,127. Among approximately 150 ECM proteins 
that were differentially expressed in normal and fibrotic kidneys, we 
identified nine core proteins that molecularly defined the fibrogenic 
niche: TNC, VTN, glutathione peroxidase 3 (GPX3), transglutami-
nase 2 (TG2), lysyl oxidase like-2 (LOXL2), serpin family H member 1 
(SERPINH1), lipopolysaccharide-binding protein (LBP), protein S100A8 
and FBN1 (Fig. 4a). The matricellular proteins TNC and VTN are pro-
duced by activated fibroblasts and macrophages, respectively, and 
have important roles in organizing the fibrogenic niche10,123. GPX3, 
also known as plasma or extracellular glutathione peroxidase, is the 
only one of the core proteins that is downregulated in CKD108. Follow-
ing kidney injury, loss of GPX3 leads to oxidative stress and thereby 
generates an extracellular microenvironment that promotes fibroblast 
activation108. TG2 and LOXL2 have roles in regulating ECM cross-linking, 
which renders ECM resistant to degradation by MMPs and contributes 
to scar formation144. Cross-linked ECM also increases tissue stiffness, 
which promotes fibroblast activation. SERPINH1, also known as HSP47, 
belongs to the serpin superfamily and has been identified as a molecu-
lar chaperone that is required for the proper folding and secretion of 
collagen proteins145. LBP and S100A8 have prominent roles in regulat-
ing inflammatory responses146,147, whereas FBN1 triggers peritubular 
endothelial cell apoptosis, leading to microvascular rarefaction127. 
Taken together, this core set of signature proteins defines an oxida-
tively stressed, profibrotic, pro-inflammatory, and anti-angiogenetic 
microenvironment for fibroblast activation.
ECM components such as matricellular proteins can bind to and 
recruit soluble factors from the surrounding milieu, creating a microen-
vironment in which profibrotic factors are enriched and concentrated 
(Fig. 4b). We showed that TNC-enriched decellularized kidney tissue 
scaffolds can recruit Wnt ligands from the surrounding media and 
thereby generate an ECM niche with high levels of these ligands148. 
The membranes of EVs contain ECM receptor integrins, suggesting 
that matricellular proteins might also recruit EVs from the extracel-
lular fluid and the circulation. Matricellular proteins could thereby 
function as a ‘molecular sponge’ that may attract and recruit soluble 
factors and EVs from the surrounding locale in a retrograde manner. 
The fibrogenic niche may therefore act as a signal exchange hub and 
provide an auspicious setting for fibroblast activation.
Intercellular communication
Although activated fibroblasts are the primary effector cells of organ 
fibrosis, they are not present in isolation. In the fibrogenic niche, 

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
623
Review article
fibroblasts engage in complex interactions with injured parenchy-
mal cells, infiltrating inflammatory cells and vascular endothe-
lial cells7,124. After injury, these cells can facilitate fibroblast activation 
through the secretion of soluble factors, release of EVs, and deposi-
tion of matricellular proteins. In turn, activated fibroblasts influence 
neighbouring cells through diverse mechanisms and promote cellular 
senescence, inflammatory responses and tissue remodelling, thereby 
providing crucial feedback mechanisms77 (Fig. 4c).

Fig. 4 | The fibrogenic niche for fibroblast activation. a, Analysis of 
decellularized mouse kidney tissue scaffolds identified a core set of nine 
extracellular matrix (ECM) proteins that are essential for establishing the 
fibrogenic niche for fibroblast activation. These proteins have roles in oxidative 
stress, inflammation, endothelial cell apoptosis and regulation of the ECM. 
b, Matricellular proteins can recruit and concentrate soluble factors and 
exosomes from the surrounding milieu in a retrograde fashion, creating a special 
microenvironment with a high level of profibrotic mediators. c, Intercellular 
communication has an important role in organ fibrogenesis. Intense crosstalk 
involving various mediators takes place between activated fibroblasts and 
injured parenchymal cells such as kidney tubular epithelial cells, infiltrated 
inflammatory cells such as monocytes or macrophages, and vascular endothelial 
cells. Inflammation-associated fibroblasts also have a role in mediating crosstalk 
between fibroblasts and inflammatory cells. Partial epithelial–mesenchymal 
transition (EMT) represents an intermediate and reversible state during which 
cells simultaneously exhibit epithelial and mesenchymal characteristics. Part a 
adapted with permission from ref. 143, Elsevier.

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
624
Review article
liver and cardiomyocytes in the heart, are usually at the epicentre of 
the damage and often serve as the initial responders to injury149. In 
cases of severe or recurring injury, injured parenchymal cells undergo 
maladaptive repair and secrete multiple bioactive substances, such 
as profibrotic and inflammatory factors, exosomes and metabolites 
including lactic acid, thereby promoting fibroblast activation through 
paracrine mechanisms149. For example, kidney tubular cells secrete 
profibrotic factors such as TGFβ1, Wnt, Shh, FGF2 and Ang II, which 
activate fibroblasts and upregulate fibrosis-related genes94,101. In addi-
tion, EVs released from stressed tubular cells transport profibrotic 
biomolecules, including Shh ligand, TGFβ mRNA and miR-21, which 
contribute to fibroblast activation11,121. Metabolic reprogramming in 
tubular epithelial cells after kidney injury is characterized by increased 
anaerobic glycolysis and lactic acid accumulation149,150. Lactic acid 
promotes fibroblast activation, which facilitates tissue repair in early 
kidney injury but induces fibrosis in the later stages149.
As mentioned above, fibroblast activation is an evolutionarily 
conserved response that is initially reparative and required for paren-
chymal cell repair and regeneration after organ damage4,5. Communi-
cation between parenchymal cells and fibroblasts is bidirectional and 
reciprocal. For example, transient activation of fibroblasts contributes 
to tubular repair through the secretion of Wnt ligands4,151 and the pro-
duction and release of retinoic acid152, erythropoietin (EPO)153, TNC126 
and HGF5. In the early stage of kidney injury, renal fibroblasts produce 
EPO under hypoxic conditions. EPO binds tubular epithelial EPO recep-
tors, triggering JAK-2–PI3K–IκB phosphorylation to activate down-
stream pathways that suppress apoptosis and enhance autophagy153. 
Fibroblast-derived HGF engages the c-Met receptor on tubular epi-
thelial cells and inhibits their apoptosis, promotes their proliferation, 
and blocks TGFβ1 signalling5. However, following chronic injury in 
CKD, fibroblasts transform into myofibroblasts, which reduces the 
production and secretion of EPO and HGF and thereby contributes to 
tubular damage149. The outcomes of the complex interactions between 
parenchymal cells and activated fibroblasts seem to be dynamic and 
changeable.
Inflammatory cells and fibroblast activation
A close connection exists between inflammation and fibrosis, and 
fibroblast activation is an inevitable consequence of unresolved 
inflammation48. The relationship between macrophages and fibroblast 
activation is of particular interest. Macrophages exhibit phenotypic heter-
ogeneity and plasticity49,123. M1 macrophages have been found to promote 
fibroblast activation154,155 or induce fibroblast apoptosis156, whereas M2 
macrophages are generally considered to activate fibroblasts, promote 
collagen I and α-SMA expression and thereby contribute to ECM remod-
elling and tissue fibrosis48. M2 macrophages also produce and secrete 
VTN123. However, emerging evidence suggests the existence of special-
ized and hybrid macrophage phenotypes beyond the traditional M1/M2 
dichotomy48,154,157. For example, in liver fibrosis, LyC6high macrophages, 
characterized by the secretion of TGFβ, TNFα, MCP1, CCL2 and CCL5, 
promote fibroblast activation, whereas LyC6low macrophages, which 
express IGF1, MMP9, MMP12, MMP13 and CX3CR1, inhibit HSC activation 
and promote fibrosis resolution158. A single nuclei RNA-sequencing study 
of human kidney allografts delineated complex immune and kidney cell 
interactions in allograft fibrosis, and identified two distinct states of 
fibrosis characterized by differences in ECM deposition and proportions 
of immune cell types159. Integration of scRNA-seq and spatial transcrip-
tomic data with lineage tracing enables deeper, more precise analysis 
of cellular subpopulations and macrophage–fibroblast interactions.
The crosstalk between macrophages and fibroblasts is bidirec-
tional, and fibroblast subsets with conserved inflammatory properties 
have been identified following injury in various organs and species160,161. 
Cytokines such as IL-1β and TNF can induce fibroblast polarization 
into inflammatory phenotypes characterized by the secretion of 
chemokines and inflammatory factors, although the expression of 
these markers varies spatially and temporally161. A scRNA-seq analysis 
identified CXCL10+CCL19+ inflammatory fibroblasts that mediated 
inflammatory responses, immune modulation and immune cell recruit-
ment and/or activation in four chronic inflammatory diseases: rheu-
matoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, interstitial lung disease 
and Sjögren’s syndrome160,162,163. Inflammatory fibroblast subsets are 
recognized to be critical coordinators that balance tissue repair and 
progression of fibrosis164.
Activated fibroblasts also recruit circulating monocytes or resident 
macrophages to the fibrogenic niche by secreting CCL2, CCL5 (ref. 165) 
and intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (ICAM-1), E-selectin and CD44 
(ref. 157). The heterogeneous fibroblast population releases diverse 
mediators that drive macrophage polarization towards different pheno-
types48. Moreover, activated fibroblasts produce abundant ECM proteins 
such as TNC, attract macrophages and facilitate ECM-mediated cell–cell 
communication77,154,166. The interaction between fibroblasts and mac-
rophages relies on temporal and spatial coordination154. Other inflam-
matory cells such as neutrophils and lymphocytes also interact with 
fibroblasts and regulate their activation167,168.
Endothelial injury and fibroblast activation
In solid organs, injured endothelial cells can promote fibroblast activa-
tion and fibrosis through various mediators. In fibrotic liver, sinusoidal 
endothelial cells (LSECs) lose fenestrations and secrete profibrotic 
factors such as TGFβ1, PDGF, ET1 and hedgehog ligands, which drive 
HSCs to differentiate into myofibroblasts157,158,169. LSEC injury causes 
oxygen diffusion abnormalities, tissue hypoxia and oxidative stress, 
leading to HIF-1α expression and enhanced fibroblast activation157,158. 
By contrast, LSECs in healthy liver maintain HSC quiescence and pre-
vent their transformation into myofibroblasts via a VEGF-stimulated 
NO-dependent or -independent pathway158. Biomechanical cues and 
exosomes also participate in mediating LSEC activation of HSCs157. In 
CKD, vascular endothelial cell apoptosis results in capillary rarefac-
tion, hypoxia and oxidative stress, which activates fibroblasts through 
HIF-1α signalling170,171. Similar endothelial-driven communication with 
fibroblasts has been observed in other fibrotic organs, such as skin172.
Fibroblasts and myofibroblasts secrete bioactive factors and 
ECM, influencing capillary structure and function. Upon activation, 
they produce TSP1, which has anti-angiogenic effects via inhibition of 
endothelial proliferation and triggering of apoptosis170,171. Cytokines 
released by fibroblasts, such as VEGF, PDGF and FGF2, have a dual 
effect on capillary stability and fibrosis170. In the early stage after injury, 
fibroblast-secreted VEGF fosters endothelial survival and growth, 
thereby aiding in tissue healing170,173. However, during fibrosis in the 
later stage after injury, loss of VEGF expression in activated fibroblasts 
exacerbates endothelial damage and capillary rarefaction170,173.
Pericytes, which are key myofibroblast precursors, closely interact 
with endothelial cells to preserve capillary function and release angi-
opoietin 1 (Ang-1) for microvascular stability169,173,174. Moreover, signal-
ling between pericytes and endothelial cells via the receptor tyrosine 
kinase EphB4 and its ligand ephrinB2 is important to stabilize the vas-
culature, and interruption of this signalling results in pericyte detach-
ment, myofibroblastic transformation and capillary rarefaction171,175. 

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
625
Review article
In CKD, pericyte injury and transformation into myofibroblasts results 
in the loss of Ang-1 (ref. 174) and interruption of EphrinB2–EphB4 
signalling171,175, leading to endothelial damage, apoptosis and increased 
capillary rarefaction.
Fate of activated fibroblasts
As the primary culprits in fibrogenesis, the fate of activated fibroblasts 
determines the outcome of tissue remodelling, injury repair and organ 
fibrosis. The size of the activated fibroblast pool after tissue injury 
results from the dynamic balance of fibroblast activation and deple-
tion. Fibroblast activation has long been the focus of countless studies 
in the field of organ fibrosis, whereas relatively little is known about 
the destiny of these cells. The fates of activated fibroblasts in fibrotic 
organs presumably include apoptosis, senescence, dedifferentiation 
and reprogramming (Fig. 5).
Apoptosis
Activated fibroblasts may be depleted via apoptosis, a form of pro-
grammed cell death that is regulated by either the extrinsic, death 
receptor-mediated pathway or the intrinsic, mitochondria-mediated 
pathway. Apoptosis results from an imbalance between pro-apoptotic 
and pro-survival factors176. Activated fibroblasts were once consid-
ered to be resistant to apoptosis132,177. However, studies have shown 
that myofibroblasts are primed for death as their mitochondria are 
loaded with pro-apoptotic BH3 proteins132,177. This loading creates an 
‘addiction’ to anti-apoptotic proteins to block pro-apoptotic signalling 
and ensure survival132,177. Furthermore, treatment with a BH3 mimetic 
induced fibroblast apoptosis and reversed established fibrosis in a 
mouse model of scleroderma178.
During physiological tissue repair, ECM softening and the release 
of pro-apoptotic factors such as IL-1β and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) 
can induce apoptosis of activated fibroblasts, leading to fibrosis 
regression132. By contrast, during organ fibrosis, ECM stiffening and 
persistent activation of profibrotic signals such as TGFβ and ET-1 result 
in upregulation of pro-survival proteins, including Bcl-2 and Bcl-xL, 
and inhibition of the apoptotic effectors Bax, Bak and caspase-3/7, 
thus preventing fibroblast apoptosis132,177. Exosomes that originate 
from stressed or injured kidney tubular cells have a pivotal role in 
dictating fibroblast apoptosis and fate by releasing TNFα-induced pro-
tein 8 (TNFAIP8)179. Exosomal TNFAIP8 promotes p53 ubiquitination, 
which leads to the degradation of p53 and thereby prolongs fibroblast 
lifespan179. Parenchymal cells may therefore have a critical role in con-
trolling the size of the activated fibroblast population during organ 
fibrosis by shuttling TNFAIP8-encapsulated EVs.
Senescence
Activated fibroblasts may undergo cellular senescence, which has a 
role in regulating fibrosis regression in multiple organs176,180. Senescent

Fig. 5 | The fate of activated fibroblasts. The fates of activated fibroblasts 
in fibrotic organs presumably include persistence, apoptosis, senescence, 
dedifferentiation and reprogramming. Whether activated fibroblasts undergo 
apoptosis depends on the balance between pro-apoptotic and anti-apoptotic 
factors. Fibroblasts may also undergo cellular senescence, which can be induced 
by various mediators including CCN1, IL-22, IL-10, IGF-1 and miR-34a. Induction of 
inactivation-associated transcription factors, such as PPARγ, GATA6, MyoD and 
TCF21, controls the dedifferentiation and inactivation of fibroblasts. In response 
to the regulation of specific transcription factors, activated fibroblasts may 
undergo lineage reprogramming and transdifferentiate into other cell types, 
such as adipocytes, cardiomyocytes and hepatocytes. All apoptotic and some 
senescent fibroblasts are removed by immune cells, enabling tissue repair 
and/or fibrosis resolution. Dedifferentiated or reprogrammed fibroblasts also 
contribute to fibrosis resolution. However, senescent or activated fibroblasts 
that escape immune clearance can survive, leading to scar formation and 
aggravated organ fibrosis.

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
626
Review article
myofibroblasts are α-SMA positive, with a senescent phenotype 
characterized by cell-cycle withdrawal, macromolecular damage, 
a senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) and dysregu-
lated metabolism176,181. Fibroblast senescence can be induced by 
various mediators including CCN1, IL-22, IL-10, IGF-1 and miR-34a176, 
mainly via telomere-dependent p53–p21 and telomere-independent 
p16 pathways63. These senescent fibroblasts contribute to fibrosis 
regression by producing ECM-degrading enzymes, decreasing ECM 
production176 and recruiting immune cells, particularly natural 
killer cells180, to induce immune clearance.
Emerging evidence suggests that fibroblast senescence can also 
exacerbate fibrosis through several mechanisms182. The SASP contains 
pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-6 and IL-8, profi-
brotic growth factors including TGFβ and PDGF, and ECM proteins 
including fibronectin and collagen182, which can promote tissue fibro-
sis. Furthermore, senescent fibroblasts can activate pro-survival mech-
anisms, such as upregulation of Bcl-xL and thereby inhibit apoptosis182. 
In aged mice with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, the expression of 
uncoupling protein-2 (UCP2) was increased, and silencing of UCP2 
inhibited fibroblast senescence and promoted apoptosis, thus alle-
viating fibrosis183. Moreover, senescent fibroblasts exhibit impaired 
dedifferentiation184. The transcription factor myoblast determination 
protein 1 (MyoD) acts as a key switch in the differentiation and dedif-
ferentiation of myofibroblasts; however, in senescent myofibroblasts, 
increased MyoD expression failed to induce dedifferentiation and 
conferred resistance to apoptosis184. The opposing effects of fibroblast 
senescence on fibrosis regression suggest that fibroblasts maintain a 
substantial level of heterogeneity, even after senescence.
Dedifferentiation
During resolution of fibrosis, activated fibroblasts may dedifferentiate 
into an inactive phenotype176. The resulting inactive fibroblasts are simi-
lar to but distinct from quiescent fibroblasts in a physiological state. 
Inactive fibroblasts express inactivation-associated genes such as SOX 
but lack certain quiescence-associated proteins, including glial fibril-
lary acidic protein (GFAP), Adipor1, adipocyte differentiation-related 
protein (ADFP) and vitamin D-binding protein (DBP)9, exhibit low prolif-
eration rates and elevated MMP activity185, and show increased sensitiv-
ity to fibrogenic stimuli9,185. A variety of biochemical and biomechanical 
cues upregulate inactivation-associated transcription factors, such as 
PPARγ9, GATA6, MyoD184 and TCF21 (refs. 176,185), which are involved in 
the dedifferentiation and inactivation of activated fibroblasts182. BMP7, 
FGF1, vitamin D and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) have been shown to induce 
inactivation of hepatic myofibroblasts176, whereas IL-1β and TNF-α can 
promote dedifferentiation of cardiac myofibroblasts182,186. Matrix sof-
tening has also been reported to induce myofibroblast deactivation182. 
Studies have reported that during resolution of liver fibrosis, ~50% of 
activated fibroblasts undergo apoptosis and ~50% dedifferentiate, 
acquire an inactive phenotype and escape apoptosis185. This finding 
suggests an important role of dedifferentiation of activated fibroblasts 
in the resolution of fibrosis.
Reprogramming
Activated fibroblasts exhibit remarkable plasticity and are capable 
of transdifferentiating into other cell types under physiological con-
ditions or through experimental manipulation, a process known as 
lineage reprogramming176. Myofibroblasts from mouse scalp can be 
reprogrammed into adipocytes, which is facilitated by BMP4 from 
adjacent hair follicles187. Similarly, in mice with myocardial infarction, 
cardiac fibroblasts could be reprogrammed into cardiomyocytes via 
overexpression of transcription factors including GATA4, HAND2, 
MEF2 and TBX5 (ref. 188). However, inducing lineage reprogramming 
in cardiac myofibroblasts is more challenging than reprogramming car-
diac fibroblasts. One study successfully used a combination of genetic 
and chemical factors to increase the efficiency of myofibroblast-to-
cardiomyocyte reprogramming in vivo189. Similarly, overexpression of 
the transcription factors FOXA3, HNF1A, HNF4A and GATA4 induced 
a proportion of hepatic myofibroblasts (0.9–2.5%) to convert into 
hepatocytes, thereby ameliorating liver fibrosis in mice190. Despite the 
scarcity of spontaneous lineage reprogramming in mammals, directed 
reprogramming of myofibroblasts may emerge as a novel therapeutic 
strategy against organ fibrosis176,182.
The ultimate fate of activated fibroblasts is either depletion or 
persistent survival, which determines the outcomes of tissue repair and 
organ fibrosis after injury. All apoptotic and some senescent fibroblasts 
are cleared by immune cells, accompanied by degradation of the ECM, 
leading to tissue repair and fibrosis resolution. Moreover, dedifferenti-
ated or reprogrammed fibroblasts undergo a phenotypic switch with 
a decrease in ECM proteins and α-SMA expression, which also contrib-
utes to the resolution of fibrosis. However, dedifferentiated fibroblasts 
sometimes become more sensitive and responsive to fibrotic stimuli, 
exhibiting a ‘memory’ of the fibrotic phenotype and priming the tissue 
for fibrosis upon reinjury. Furthermore, a proportion of senescent or 
activated fibroblasts that escape apoptosis and immune clearance 
can survive, leading to scar formation and aggravated organ fibrosis.
Therapeutic intervention
As activated fibroblasts are the principal effector cells in organ fibrosis, 
developing strategies to target these cells is central to the therapy of 
many chronic fibrotic diseases. Inhibiting fibroblast activation during 
the early stages of injury could prevent the occurrence and progression 
of fibrosis, whereas inducing their depletion in the later stages could 
facilitate the resolution of fibrosis. Potential therapeutic strategies 
include disrupting the formation of the fibrogenic niche, inhibiting 
fibroblast activation, promoting fibroblast depletion and accelerating 
fibrosis resolution176,185 (Fig. 6).
Disrupting formation of the fibrogenic niche
As formation of the fibrogenic niche is a fundamental initial step in 
organ fibrosis, disrupting its formation is a potential therapeutic strat-
egy for fibrotic diseases124. Formation of the fibrogenic niche could 
potentially be disrupted by blocking the function or expression of 
key matricellular proteins. We have utilized decellularized kidney tis-
sue scaffolds as a screening platform to identify small-molecule com-
pounds that could disrupt the formation of the fibrogenic niche124. 
Alternatively, monoclonal antibodies could be designed to specifi-
cally inhibit the activity of matricellular proteins or peptides could 
be designed to interfere with the interactions between matricellular 
proteins and their binding partners, thereby disrupting their func-
tion. Similarly, biomaterials such as nanoparticles or hydrogels could 
be designed to incorporate inhibitors of matricellular proteins and 
engineered to control the release and targeting specificity of these 
drugs with the aim of disrupting formation of the fibrogenic niche.
Although disrupting formation of the fibrogenic niche by targeting 
matricellular proteins is a promising therapeutic strategy for fibrotic 
diseases, a phase III randomized clinical trial of an anti-CTGF antibody 
did not show a beneficial effect in patients with idiopathic pulmonary 
fibrosis191. The specificity, safety and efficacy of such approaches need 

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
627
Review article
to be carefully evaluated through preclinical and clinical studies to 
ensure their therapeutic potential in a disease-specific context.
Targeted inhibition of fibroblast activation
Targeted inhibition of fibroblast activation is a potential therapeutic 
strategy for fibrotic diseases. As discussed above, fibroblast activation 
is controlled by numerous growth factors and signalling pathways7,77. 
In particular, the TWA cycle consisting of TGFβ, Wnt and Ang II is a 
core signalling network that drives organ fibrosis118. Simultaneously 
targeting different elements of the TWA cycle may be a more effective 
therapeutic approach to fibrosis than a monotherapy that targets a sin-
gle signalling pathway. For example, Klotho has been shown to inhibit 
TGFβ, Wnt–β-catenin and Ang II95,192,193 and to ameliorate kidney fibrosis 
in mouse models of IRI and folic acid-induced AKI to CKD transition194,195 
However, use of Klotho as a therapeutic agent is difficult because it 
is a large and complex transmembrane protein. To circumvent this 
problem, we developed Klotho-derived peptides, KP1 and KP6, which 
block TGFβ and Wnt signalling, respectively, and could potentially 
mitigate kidney fibrosis in CKD111,196,197.
Although not initially developed as anti-fibrosis therapies7,198, 
RAAS blockers, SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists and endothe-
lin-1 receptor antagonists have been demonstrated to inhibit fibroblast 
activation and slow the progression of CKD7. In preclinical studies, 
targeting Wnt–β-catenin signalling using ICG-001 also ameliorated kid-
ney fibrosis199. These results indicate that targeted inhibition of major 
profibrotic signalling pathways could suppress fibroblast activation, 
leading to alleviation of fibrotic lesions.
Promoting fibroblast depletion
Another strategy for the therapeutic intervention of fibrotic diseases is 
to enhance fibroblast depletion via induction of apoptosis, dedifferen-
tiation or reprogramming. This approach might have the potential to 
reverse established fibrosis by modulating the fate of activated fibro-
blasts. BH3 mimetics that selectively antagonize anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 
proteins have been shown to activate the intrinsic apoptosis pathway 
in activated fibroblasts132,177,182. Several BH3 mimetics are currently in 
clinical trials, including ABT-263 (navitoclax), which blocks Bcl-2, Bcl-xL 
and Bcl-W, and A-1331852, which is a specific inhibitor of Bcl-xL132,177,178.

Fig. 6 | Potential therapeutic interventions for fibrotic diseases. a, Disrupting 
formation of the fibrogenic niche by targeting matricellular proteins using small 
molecule inhibitors, specific antibodies, peptides or matrikines. b, Inhibiting 
fibroblast activation by targeting key profibrotic mediators and their signalling 
pathways, particularly the TGFβ–Wnt–Ang II (TWA) cycle. c, Promoting depletion 
of fibroblasts by inducing their apoptosis, dedifferentiation or reprogramming 
and eliminating senescent fibroblasts. d, Modulating tissue stiffness and 
promoting fibrosis resolution by targeting matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), 
inhibiting lysyl oxidase like-2 (LOXL2) or targeting the plasminogen activator 
inhibitor PAI-1. e, Using mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) therapy, the MSC 
secretome or MSC-derived exosomes to foster a reparative or regenerative 
environment and re-establish tissue homeostasis.

Nature Reviews Nephrology | Volume 21 | September 2025 | 613–632
628
Review article
An engineered human TNF-related apoptosis-inducing ligand, TLY012, 
has been shown to selective activate death receptor 5 (DR5) and thereby 
activate the extrinsic pathway of apoptosis in myofibroblasts182,200. Trig-
gering fibroblast apoptosis may hold promise as a novel antifibrotic 
therapy. However, the specificity and efficacy of these treatments 
remain to be validated.
Senolytic agents, such as dasatinib and quercetin, selectively 
eliminate senescent cells182. Combined treatment with dasatinib and 
quercetin was shown to reduce lung fibrosis in a mouse model of idi-
opathic pulmonary fibrosis132. Senolytic agents typically target all 
senescent cells without distinguishing between senescent epithe-
lial cells and fibroblasts176. As senescent fibroblasts seem to have some 
beneficial actions in promoting tissue repair and preventing fibrosis, 
timing of the intervention is likely crucial to the success of senolytic 
fibroblast-targeted therapy132.
Although spontaneous lineage reprogramming of fibroblasts 
rarely occurs, induction of fibroblast dedifferentiation and reprogram-
ming may offer a novel strategy for antifibrotic therapy. Various drugs 
can promote the dedifferentiation of activated fibroblasts, including 
PPARγ agonists and vitamin D receptor (VDR) agonists9. The transdiffer-
entiation of activated fibroblasts into other cell types through lineage 
reprogramming represents a potential route of fibrosis resolution176. 
Overexpression of specific combinations of transcription factors 
in activated fibroblasts in fibrotic organs can induce lineage repro-
gramming. For example, in a mouse model of liver cirrhosis, in vivo 
reprogamming of myofibroblasts was able to attenuate hepatic fibrosis 
induced by up to 8 weeks of repeated Ccl4 injections176.
Modulating tissue stiffness and fibrosis resolution
As fibroblast activation is controlled by ECM stiffness, therapies target-
ing ECM-modulating enzymes could potentially inhibit fibroblast acti-
vation and resolve organ fibrosis9. The lysyl oxidase family, including 
LOXL2, which is induced in fibrotic organs but not present in normal tis-
sues, promotes collagen crosslinking and stiffening, thereby hindering 
fibrosis resolution9,176. LOXL2 inhibitors, such as AB0023 (simtuzumab) 
and PAT-1251, have shown promise in alleviating experimental fibrosis 
but no effect in clinical trials9.
MMPs not only regulate ECM remodelling but also participate in 
controlling various biological processes201. MMP-7 and MMP-10, which 
are elevated in kidney fibrosis and serve as key mediators in CKD, are 
potential targets for resolving fibrosis93,202. The plasminogen activa-
tor, which catalyses the conversion of plasminogen into plasmin, is 
controlled by its inhibitor PAI-1, indicating its potential as a therapeutic 
target9. However, the multifunctionality of ECM-modulating enzymes 
suggests that these therapies may also lead to immune responses or 
adverse effects. The specificity and efficacy of these approaches need 
to be carefully scrutinized.
Fostering a reparative or regenerative environment
In many respects, tissue fibrosis can be viewed as a process of failed 
repair after injury. Stem cell therapy to boost tissue repair and regen-
eration has emerged as a promising antifibrotic strategy. Infusion 
of autologous or allogenic MSCs has been shown to enhance tissue 
repair and/or recovery and reduce fibrosis in numerous preclinical and 
clinical studies203. Therapeutic efficacy of intravenous MSC injection 
has been shown in patients with pulmonary fibrosis and liver cirrho-
sis, without severe adverse effects203–205. The homing and paracrine 
signalling capacities of MSCs have been leveraged for drug delivery. 
For example, researchers have developed nanoengineered drug carriers 
by bioconjugating MSCs with type I collagenase-modified liposomes 
loaded with nintedanib and have shown that exosomes derived from 
human adipose MSCs that were transfected with glial cell-derived 
neurotrophic factor (GDNF) ameliorated renal fibrosis in a unilat-
eral ureteral obstruction model206,207. However, long-term safety 
concerns persist regarding immune rejection and tumorigenic risks, 
along with unresolved differentiation disparities between endog-
enous and exogenous MSCs204. These issues have led to the pursuit 
of safer cell-free alternatives such as MSC-conditioned medium, 
which contains the complete secretome204, and MSC exosomes that 
can deliver antifibrotic molecules with modifiable targeting capac-
ity via surface engineering208. These approaches could be used to 
modulate stem cell–fibroblast interactions and re-establish tissue 
homeostasis in the context of fibrosis therapy. Future armaments 
in the fight against organ fibrosis could include strategies to foster 
stem cell-mediated repair and a regenerative environment.
Conclusions
Activated fibroblasts are a specialized cell type that arises in response 
to tissue injury and has a central role in the pathogenesis of fibrotic 
diseases, which impact many organ systems and contribute to severe 
morbidity and high mortality worldwide. Activated fibroblasts origi-
nate from different precursors in different organs, including interstitial 
fibroblasts, vascular pericytes and bone marrow-derived progeni-
tor cells. Activated fibroblasts are not a homogenous population but 
consist of different subpopulations with distinct characteristics and 
functions. The plasticity and heterogeneity of activated fibroblasts 
may be attributable to differences in their origin and distinct stages 
during their activation. Advances in scRNA-seq and spatial transcrip-
tomics have enabled fibroblast subpopulations to be better defined 
and improved the understanding of fibroblast biology.
Sustained activation of fibroblasts is a central event in the evo-
lution of organ fibrosis that deserves special attention. Among the 
many profibrotic factors that have been identified, TGFβ, Wnts and 
Ang II are of particular importance and constitute a core signalling 
network, the TWA cycle, that drives fibroblast activation and organ 
fibrosis in a variety of disease settings. In addition to soluble factors, 
fibroblast activation is controlled by many other extracellular media-
tors such as matricellular proteins, EVs, and biomechanical cues. These 
findings provide novel insights into the molecular mechanisms that 
govern fibroblast activation and have important implications for the 
development of therapeutics for fibrotic diseases.
Activated fibroblasts seem to be highly plastic, with a multitude 
of different fates, including apoptosis, senescence, dedifferentiation 
and lineage reprogramming, that may determine the final outcome of 
fibrotic disorders. A multidisciplinary approach is essential to develop 
targeted therapies that can halt or reverse fibroblast activation and 
re-establish tissue homeostasis, ultimately improving outcomes for 
patients with fibrotic diseases. More in-depth studies of fibroblast biol-
ogy hold the key to addressing the challenges posed by organ fibrosis 
and improving the quality of life of millions of affected individuals 
worldwide.

===== Zhao2016_Biosci_AnimalRegeneration =====
Overview Articles
http://bioscience.oxfordjournals.org	
September 2016 / Vol. 66 No. 9 • BioScience   735 
What Determines the Regenerative 
Capacity in Animals?
ANDONG ZHAO, HUA QIN, AND XIAOBING FU
The regenerative phenomenon is widespread, but regenerative capacity varies greatly across animals. Invertebrates and phylogenetically 
primitive vertebrates, such as salamanders and zebrafish, often possess a higher regenerative capacity than mammals have. Even in the same 
individual, different tissues or organs exhibit distinct regenerative capacity; for example, livers regenerate more readily than hearts in mammals. 
In addition, the younger animal is usually easier to regenerate than the older. Decades of research are beginning to yield explanations about why 
regenerative capacity differs markedly, based on cellular and molecular components and evolutionary ideas. Here, we discuss several reasons 
for differences in regenerative capacity, including the properties of stem cells, dedifferentiation and transdifferentiation potentials, expression of 
regeneration-associated genes, epigenetic regulators, and immune responses. Comprehensive analyses of these perspectives would provide new 
insights into how to promote regeneration in mammals.
Keywords: regeneration, stem cell, dedifferentiation, epigenetic, immune response
R
egeneration occurs widely in the animal kingdom, 
 although their regenerative capacity varies consider­
ably. Invertebrates can regenerate the entire organisms (e.g., 
planarians and Hydra; Wittlieb et al. 2006, van Wolfswinkel 
et al. 2014). Phylogenetically primitive vertebrates, such as 
amphibians and fish, are capable of regenerating substantial 
parts of their body but not an entire organism. Urodele 
amphibians (salamanders) have a remarkable capability 
to regenerate a wide array of tissues and organs, includ­
ing limbs, tails, jaws, spinal cords, and lenses. Similar to 
salamanders, teleost fish (zebrafish) can regrow hearts, 
fins, lenses, retinas, spinal cords, and so forth. By contrast, 
mammals have a very limited regenerative capacity. Severe 
damage to tissues or organs (e.g., hearts, limbs, or spinal 
cords) does not induce regenerative responses but rather a 
simple healing concomitant with fibrotic scarring. These 
collectively indicate that the capacity to regenerate gener­
ally decreases during evolutionary development. Moreover, 
regenerative capacity has a tendency to decline during onto­
genic development or with age. Two classic examples are 
the transition from the fetal scarless wound healing to the 
typical adult scarring repair in mammals (Larson et al. 2010) 
and the gradual loss of limb regeneration from the larval 
stage to the adult in anuran amphibians (e.g., frogs; Mescher 
and Neff 2005). In the same individual, different tissues or 
organs display diverse degrees of regeneration: Livers regen­
erate more readily than hearts in mammals (Fausto et  al. 
2006, Porrello et al. 2011). Regenerative capacity is of great 
interest to scientists; why regenerative capacity differs across 
animals and tissues has been under continual investigation 
for several decades.
Given that cells are the foundation of regeneration, the 
availability of abundant cellular sources will inevitably 
determine the regenerative capacity. There are generally 
three mechanisms of yielding new cells in vivo, including 
the activation of stem or progenitor cells, the reversion of 
differentiated cells to their progenitors, and the conversion 
of one tissue cell into another (Jopling et al. 2011). The lat­
ter two are called dedifferentiation and transdifferentiation. 
Regenerative species either keep amounts of adult stem cells 
in their body or have a huge potential to undergo dedifferen­
tiation and transdifferentiation in their adult cells. However, 
the three mechanisms are lacking in most adult mammals, 
which largely limits their regenerative capacity. The selected 
expression or silencing of regeneration-associated genes 
also affects regeneration. These genes might be possessed or 
expressed exclusively in regenerative species but not in non­
regenerative species. In recent years, accumulating evidence 
strongly suggests that epigenetic regulators exert enormous 
influence on regeneration by modulating various aspects 
of regeneration processes (Cho et  al. 2013, Gornikiewicz 
et  al. 2013, Powell et  al. 2013). Moreover, there has been 
a growing appreciation of contributions of the immune 
system to regeneration (Mescher and Neff 2005, Aurora 
and Olson 2014). The inflammatory microenvironment 
mediated by the immune response is essential for cell sur­
vival, growth, and function. Proper immune responses cre­
ate a regeneration-permissive microenvironment, whereas 
BioScience 66: 735–746. © The Author(s) 2016. Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of the American Institute of Biological Sciences. All rights 
reserved. For Permissions, please e-mail: journals.permissions@oup.com. 
doi:10.1093/biosci/biw079
Advance Access publication 20 July 2016
Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/bioscience/article/66/9/735/1753631 by guest on 02 March 2021

Overview Articles
736   BioScience • September 2016 / Vol. 66 No. 9	
http://bioscience.oxfordjournals.org
aberrant immune responses cause a detrimental, inflamma­
tory microenvironment that impedes regeneration.
Here, in light of recent literature, we first describe several 
well-studied regeneration models with emphasis on the cel­
lular origins of regeneration. Then, we discuss the reasons 
for the differences in the regenerative capacity of animals 
at many levels, including the cellular mechanism, gene 
expression, epigenetic regulation, and immune response. 
We bring together common elements affecting regeneration 
and compare their contributions to regeneration. Our aim is 
to provide new insights into how to promote regeneration 
in mammals.
Regeneration models in invertebrates 
and the cellular basis
Planarians are tiny flatworms with the ability to regrow 
the entire organisms (figure 1a). This capacity depends on 
the abundant reserve of adult stem cells (throughout their 
bodies; neoblasts are small (5–8 μm in diameter), highly 
undifferentiated cells, and by morphology, they represent 
approximately 25%–30% of all planarian cells. In response 
to injury, neoblasts accumulate to form a regeneration blas­
tema and then convert into any cell type required for regen­
eration. This pluripotency of neoblasts is similar to that of 
embryonic stem cells in mammals. Accordingly, neoblasts 
were long thought to be a homogeneous population of adult 
pluripotent stem cells. Consistently, Wagner and colleagues 
(2011) identified a subpopulation of neoblasts that can 
form large descendant-cell colonies and give birth to any 
cell type within the body. The subpopulation is described 
as clonogenic neoblasts (cNeoblasts). Transplantation of a 
single cNeoblast could rescue the regeneration in irradi­
ated planarians, suggesting cNeoblasts have the potential to 
regenerate a whole body. Based on multidimensional single-
cell transcriptional profiling, however, a recent study has 
demonstrated that neoblasts are indeed heterogeneous, con­
sisting of the pluripotent subpopulation (sigma-neoblasts) 
and the lineage-restricted progenitor subpopulation (zeta-
neoblasts) (van Wolfswinkel et al. 2014). Zeta-neoblasts, 
as committed progenitor cells, can only yield postmitotic 
lineages, including epidermal cells, but they do not contrib­
ute to regeneration. Sigma-neoblasts are able to differentiate 
into any cell type (including zeta-neoblasts) and mainly 
responsible for regeneration. In addition, the cNeoblasts are 
likely contained in the sigma-neoblasts (van Wolfswinkel 
et al. 2014). Therefore, neoblasts are a mixed mass compris­
ing pluripotent stem cells and lineage-restricted progenitor 
cells, although regeneration primarily depends on the plu­
ripotent subpopulation.
Hydra is a member of the animal phylum Cnidaria, liv­
ing in freshwater. The animal has a polarized, primary body 
axis and has two epithelial cell layers. Like planarians, Hydra 
exhibits strikingly high regeneration: When it is cut in half, 
the top half regenerates a foot, and the bottom half regener­
ates a head (figure 1b). Although Hydra is devoid of pluripo­
tent stem cells, it has three stem cell types (ectodermal 
and endodermal epithelial stem cells and interstitial stem 
cells) throughout the body (figure 2b). The epithelial stem 
cells contribute to the regeneration of the epidermal layers 
(Wittlieb et  al. 2006), and the interstitial stem cells con­
tribute to the regeneration of the other tissues (Hemmrich 
et al. 2012). The interstitial stem cells have the multipotent 
potential to give rise to all other cells except epithelial cells, 
including neurons, nematocytes, secretory cells, and gam­
etes. Therefore, three stem cell types together produce all cell 
types within the Hydra body, probably as the major reason 
for the unprecedented regenerative capacity.
Regeneration models in primitive vertebrates 
and the cellular basis
Urodele amphibians (salamanders) do not generate the 
entire body, but they can regrow substantial parts. Their 
limb regeneration is a well-characterized model for the 
regeneration of complex tissues. When amputated anywhere 
along the limb axis, salamanders completely regenerate the 
missing segments (figure 1c). Using lineage-tracing tools, 
Kragl and colleagues (2009) designed an elegant transplant 
experiment labeling limb tissue in axolotls (one kind of 
salamanders). They determined that the progenitors local­
ized in the each tissue of limbs migrate to form the blastema 
(Kragl et al. 2009). In effect, blastema formation does not 
involve the conversion of one tissue cell to other tissue cells. 
This study has concluded that blastema is a heterogeneous 
pool of distinct lineage-restricted progenitor cells from its 
original limb tissue.
Are these lineage-restricted progenitor cells derived from 
the dedifferentiation of mature cells or from the activation of 
resident stem cells? Although the dominant view is that blas­
tema originates from dedifferentiation, both mechanisms are 
actually implicated in blastema formation, depending on the 
species and tissues. For limb muscle regeneration in newts, 
Sandoval-Guzman and colleagues (2014) demonstrated that 
multinucleate myofibers fragment into proliferating, paired-
box protein-7 (Pax7, a marker for muscle stem cells)-nega­
tive mononucleate cells in the blastema. Subsequently, these 
dedifferentiated cells generate limb muscles on the basis of 
genetic-fate mapping (Sandoval-Guzman et al. 2014). More 
recently, studies have shown that programmed cell death 
induces myofibers to dedifferentiate into muscle progenitor 
cells in newt limbs (Wang H et al. 2015). Do muscle stem 
cells contribute to blastema or not? The muscle stem cells, 
namely the Pax7-positive satellite cells, are indeed con­
tained in the newt limb and activated to incorporate into 
the blastema after limb amputation (Morrison et al. 2006). 
Nevertheless, muscle stem cells seem to contribute rarely to 
muscle regeneration in newts, because Pax7-positive satellite 
cells are deficient in the blastema, except at the very early 
stage of blastema formation (Sandoval-Guzman et al. 2014). 
To the contrary, limb muscles in axolotls regenerate from 
activation of muscle satellite cells rather than from muscle 
dedifferentiation (Sandoval-Guzman et  al. 2014). For that 
reason, an evolutionary diversity exists in the limb muscle 
Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/bioscience/article/66/9/735/1753631 by guest on 02 March 2021
